text: The Taylor Titch is a single-seat, low-wing monoplane designed by John Taylor for the 1964 Midget Racer Design Competition, achieving second place. It first flew in 1967.

Stephen Langton (c. 1150 – 9 July 1228) was an English cardinal and Archbishop of Canterbury (1207–1228). A key figure in the dispute between King John and Pope Innocent III, he was exiled until John's submission in 1213. Langton later helped draft Magna Carta (1215) and promoted church reforms. A scholar and theologian, he is traditionally credited with dividing the Bible into modern chapters. He died in Sussex and was buried at Canterbury Cathedral.

Casey Cizikas (born February 27, 1991) is a Canadian professional ice hockey centre currently playing for the New York Islanders in the NHL. Selected 92nd overall by the Islanders in the 2009 NHL Entry Draft, Cizikas began his career in the Ontario Hockey League (OHL) with the Mississauga St. Michael's Majors, where he served as captain and led the team in scoring. He made his NHL debut in 2012 and is recognized as one of the league's top fourth-line players. Cizikas has also represented Canada internationally, winning a silver medal at the 2011 World Junior Ice Hockey Championships.

Dinkar D. Patil (6 November 1915 – 23 October 2005) was an Indian film director, scriptwriter, and author, known for his significant contributions to Marathi cinema. Active from 1948 to 1993, he directed 35 films and wrote scripts for over 60, often focusing on rural themes and social change. He introduced Marathi Lavani folk dance to cinema and played a key role in establishing the Marathi Film City in Kolhapur. Patil also authored an acclaimed autobiography, *Patlache Por*.

The Sultan of Selangor is the hereditary monarch and head of state of the Malaysian state of Selangor. Established in 1766, the Selangor Sultanate traces its lineage to a Bugis dynasty. The Sultan serves as the Head of Islam in Selangor and holds significant constitutional and ceremonial roles, including appointing the Menteri Besar and conferring state honors. The current Sultan, Sharafuddin Idris Shah, has reigned since 2001. The Sultanate's official residences include Istana Alam Shah in Klang and Istana Bukit Kayangan in Shah Alam. The Sultan of Selangor is also a member of Malaysia's Conference of Rulers, eligible to be elected as the Yang di-Pertuan Agong.
Trisha Ford (born October 19, 1977) is an American softball coach and former player, currently serving as head coach at Texas A&M.

In topology, an atlas is a collection of charts that together describe the structure of a topological space, particularly a manifold. Each chart is a homeomorphism from an open subset of the space to an open subset of Euclidean space. Transition maps between overlapping charts allow for comparisons and ensure consistency. Atlases can have additional structure, such as differentiability or smoothness, enabling the study of differentiable or smooth manifolds.

"Flash" is a song by the Japanese girl group Perfume, released as a single on March 16, 2016, and later included in their album *Cosmic Explorer*. Produced by Yasutaka Nakata, the song blends J-pop and EDM elements and features choreography inspired by martial arts and Tai chi. Its music video, directed by Yusuke Tanaka, incorporates CGI and LED light sabers, becoming Perfume's most-watched video on YouTube with over 60 million views.
Karlo Matković (born March 30, 2001) is a Bosnian-Croatian professional basketball player for Cedevita Olimpija. He was drafted by the New Orleans Pelicans in 2022.

Corbett and Courtney Before the Kinetograph (1894) is an early American silent film produced by the Edison Manufacturing Company. Directed by William K.L. Dickson, it features a staged six-round boxing match between James J. Corbett and Peter Courtney, filmed inside the Black Maria studio in New Jersey. One of the first boxing films ever made, it was intended for exhibition on the Kinetoscope. The film is now in the public domain and preserved by the Library of Congress.

The Swallow Music Awards (Armenian: Ծիծեռնակ երաժշտական մրցանակաբաշխություն) is an annual music awards ceremony in Armenia, presented by the Ministry of Culture since its inception in 2017. Held at the Armenian National Academic Theatre of Opera and Ballet, the awards recognize excellence and outstanding achievement in various music genres, including classical, folk, and pop. Unlike other Armenian music awards, the Swallow Music Awards features a nomination process based on submitted applications, with specific delegations determining winners in each genre. The awards have faced some controversy, particularly regarding the application requirement, which has drawn criticism from certain artists.
Remnantz is a historic house near Marlow, Buckinghamshire, built around 1720. It housed the Royal Military Academy from 1802 to 1811.

Old Street, historically recorded as Ealdestrate around 1200, is an ancient thoroughfare in London that likely follows a Roman or pre-Roman route. The street features a mix of architectural styles, with some surviving domestic buildings from the 18th and early 19th centuries. It serves as a significant civic hub, marked by Shoreditch Town Hall and St Leonard's Church. In recent years, Old Street has gained prominence as a center for technology and nightlife, earning the nickname "Silicon Roundabout." The area is also known for its vibrant street art, attracting notable artists like Banksy. Old Street station is located at the junction with City Road.
Peter von Hess (29 July 1792 – 4 April 1871) was a German painter known for battle scenes and paintings of the Greek War of Liberation. He received training from his father and the Munich Academy.

Polyphony in literature, a concept developed by Russian philosopher and literary theorist Mikhail Bakhtin, refers to a narrative style in which multiple, independent voices or consciousnesses interact on equal terms within a text. Unlike monologic works, where the author's ideology dominates and characters' ideas are subordinated to a unified worldview, polyphonic works allow characters to express their own "voice-ideas"—a fusion of personality and perspective—without being finalized or objectified by the author. This approach fosters open-ended dialogue and the coexistence of diverse, unmerged perspectives.

Bakhtin identified Fyodor Dostoevsky as a master of the polyphonic novel, where ideas are not abstract propositions but are embodied in the unique orientations of characters. In such works, the author relinquishes monologic control, engaging as an equal participant in the dialogue rather than imposing predetermined conclusions. This creates a dynamic, eventful narrative where truths emerge through the interaction of autonomous voices.

In contrast, monologic literature is characterized by the author's ultimate authority, where ideas are either affirmed or repudiated within a fixed ideological framework. Polyphony, however, celebrates the unfinalizable nature of human consciousness, emphasizing the creative potential of dialogue and interaction.

Barbil is a city located in the Keonjhar district of Odisha, India, with a population of approximately 300,000 as of 2017. Situated at an elevation of 1,077 meters, Barbil is surrounded by hills and is known for its rich deposits of iron and manganese ore, housing several steel and iron ore pellet plants. The official language is Odia, and the city has a diverse demographic, with a significant tribal population. Barbil is well-connected by rail and road, facilitating transport to major cities like Howrah and Bhubaneshwar.

Mickey Doyle is a fictional character in the HBO series *Boardwalk Empire*, portrayed by Paul Sparks. A Polish-American gangster and bootlegger, he operates in Atlantic City during Prohibition, frequently switching allegiances between crime bosses like Nucky Thompson and rival factions. Known for his opportunistic and often bumbling nature, Doyle plays a key role in several gang conflicts before being killed by Lucky Luciano in the final season. The character is loosely based on real-life mobster Mickey Duffy.

Giosue Giuppone was an Italian cyclist and motorcycle and automobile racer. He won Italian national championships in cycling, and raced motorcycles for Peugeot. Giuppone won the Coupe des Voiturettes in 1909. He died in 1910 while testing a car for the race.

Susan Sonnenschein is an American psychologist and educator known for her work in developmental psychology. She earned her BA in psychology from New York University, an M.S. in educational psychology from Pennsylvania State University, and a Ph.D. in developmental psychology from Stony Brook University. Since joining the psychology department at the University of Maryland, Baltimore County (UMBC) in 1979, Sonnenschein has taught various undergraduate and graduate courses, focusing on child development and assessment. She has mentored many aspiring psychologists and has served on numerous dissertation and master's committees, receiving the Donald Creighton Award for Outstanding Faculty Member in 2008.
*The Time* is a studio album by Xu Weizhou, released in 2017 and 2018. It consists of four quarters of music.

A "län" is an administrative division in Sweden, often translated as "county," while in Finland, it is referred to as a "province." The term historically means "fief" and reflects a shared administrative tradition between the two countries that ended in 1809. In Sweden, a län functions as an extension of the national government without autonomy or legislative power. Each län has a governor, known as a landshövding, who oversees the County Administrative Board. Historically, the term has evolved, with previous titles like "guberniya" used in Finland during its time as a Grand Duchy under Russia.

The Stockade Building System is a construction method patented in 1927 by Buckminster Fuller and his father-in-law, James Hewlett. It used fibrous blocks coated with plaster, molded with holes for concrete or beams to create a weight-bearing structure. It was evaluated at a residence in Florida in 1926.

Victory Day over Nazism in World War II (Ukrainian: День перемоги над нацизмом у Другій світовій війні) is a Ukrainian holiday celebrated annually on May 9. Established in 2015, it replaced the Soviet-era Victory Day, following the country's decommunization laws. The holiday aims to shift the focus from glorifying war to honoring personal histories, similar to Remembrance Day. The remembrance poppy is the official symbol.

Building officials are professionals responsible for regulating construction projects, ensuring compliance with building codes and safety standards. They typically hold qualifications in engineering, architecture, or construction management and may be certified by relevant authorities.

Shigeichi Nagano (1925 – January 30, 2019) was a Japanese photographer known for documenting Japan's postwar economic growth and urban life, particularly Tokyo's salarymen. He worked in publishing, film, and television, earning numerous awards and retrospectives.

Lakes Mall was a shopping mall in Florida that opened in 1972 and closed in 1995. Anchored by Jefferson Ward and Britt's, it struggled with declining tenants in the 1980s before being demolished in 1996. The site was later redeveloped with discount stores and educational facilities.

HMS *Ardent* was a Royal Navy torpedo boat destroyer launched in 1894 and built by Thornycroft in Chiswick. Designed as part of the Ardent-class, she served primarily in the Mediterranean, performing duties such as training stokers and participating in fleet exercises. *Ardent* achieved notable speeds during trials, exceeding 27 knots. After a collision in 1906 and subsequent service, she was placed in reserve in 1911 and sold for breaking later that year.

Hambleden Lock is a lock with a weir on the River Thames in Berkshire, England, maintained by the Environment Agency. First built in 1773 and rebuilt in 1994, it operates hydraulically and features a fall of 1.44 meters. The lock is accessible from the village of Aston and via walkways across the weirs. Historically, the site dates back to the Domesday Book, with references to a weir and flash lock as early as 1338. The reach above the lock includes notable landmarks such as Greenlands, Temple Island, and the Henley Royal Regatta course.
**Andrea Batista Schlesinger** (born October 27, 1976) is an American political writer and campaigner. She directed the Drum Major Institute from 2002 to 2009.
2017 CONCACAF U-20 Championship squads lists the head coaches of the 12 participating national teams.

"D.A.N.C.E." is a 2007 nu-disco song by French electronic duo Justice, from their debut album *†*. Inspired by Michael Jackson, it features interpolations of his songs and vocals from a London choir. The track peaked at number 4 on *Rolling Stone*'s 100 Best Songs of 2007. Its animated music video, directed by Jonas & François, won an MTV Europe Music Award.

Hire Benakal (Hirebenakallu) is a prehistoric archaeological site located in the Koppal district of Karnataka, India. Dating back over 2,000 years, the site is renowned for its megalithic monuments, including dolmens, cists, and funerary structures, which are believed to have been constructed between 800 BCE and 200 BCE during the Iron Age. Spread across a rocky terrain of seven hills near the Tungabhadra River, the site features approximately 400 megalithic monuments, many of which are arranged in circular layouts and include anthropomorphic structures, menhirs, and stone enclosures. Notable discoveries include cave paintings, pottery, and iron implements from the Neolithic and megalithic periods.

First documented in 1835 by Philip Meadows Taylor, the site has since been studied by archaeologists such as Mortimer Wheeler and Adiga Sundara. Despite its historical significance, Hire Benakal remains relatively unknown and poorly maintained, with limited preservation efforts by the Archaeological Survey of India. Local villagers attribute spiritual significance to the site, believing it to be visited by a deity during annual festivals.
Thierry Escaich (born 1965) is a French composer, organist, and improviser.

Aqua is a Danish pop group formed in Copenhagen in 1989, originally known as Joyspeed. The band consists of members René Dif, Lene Nystrøm, and Søren Rasted, with Claus Norreen as a past member. Aqua gained international fame in the late 1990s with their catchy bubblegum pop sound, particularly through their hit single "Barbie Girl," which topped charts worldwide. Their debut album, *Aquarium*, released in 1997, included other successful tracks like "Doctor Jones" and "Lollipop (Candyman)." Following a brief hiatus, Aqua reunited in 2008, releasing new music and touring. Their second album, *Aquarius*, came out in 2000, showcasing a more diverse sound. The group has experienced several reunions and tours since, including performances at music festivals and the release of new singles. Aqua's music is characterized by its upbeat melodies and playful lyrics, drawing influences from artists like ABBA and Ace of Base. As of 2022, Aqua continues to perform and release music, maintaining a dedicated fan base.

The King of Rome (Latin: rex) was the chief magistrate of the Roman Kingdom. According to tradition, the first king was Romulus, who founded the city in 753 BC. The last king was Lucius Tarquinius Superbus, who was overthrown in 509 BC, leading to the establishment of the Roman Republic. The king possessed absolute power, including military, executive, and judicial authority. He was advised by the Senate, but it had limited power. Kings were elected by the Curiate Assembly, though the Senate played a key role in the nomination process. After the abolition of the monarchy, the title of king was retained for the rex sacrorum, a priest with limited powers.
Pornsanae Sitmonchai is a Thai Muay Thai fighter known for his aggressive style and notable career, including a murder charge in 2020.

Societetsskolan, inaugurated on November 1, 1787, in Gothenburg, Sweden, was a pioneering educational institution aimed at providing equal education for both males and females, reflecting the ideals of the Moravian Church. Initially serving the children of the Moravian community, it quickly gained popularity among non-Moravians, particularly among wealthy merchant families. The school offered a structured curriculum that included subjects such as German, French, English, Geography, History, and Mathematics, making it one of the first in Sweden to provide serious secondary education to girls. Although it was not free, it employed both male and female teachers, with female educators referred to as "Aunts." Societetsskolan is often recognized as the first girls' school in Sweden to offer a more equitable education compared to other institutions of its time.

Aussie Gold Hunters is an Australian factual television series that follows gold miners across Western Australia, Victoria, and Queensland as they use various techniques—including metal detecting, sluicing, and dry blowing—to reach their seasonal gold targets. Premiering on Discovery Channel in 2016, the show has aired seven seasons and over 100 episodes. Produced by Electric Pictures, it has become a ratings success in Australia and internationally, ranking as one of Foxtel's top factual programs and a leading show on the UK's Quest channel.

Echo Lawn Estate is a historic property located in Balmville, New York, notable for its mid-19th-century Hudson River Valley estate design and Second Empire architectural style. Built around 1848, the estate features a brick mansion with a mansard roof, complemented by Picturesque-style service buildings, including a carriage barn, gatehouse, stable, and ice house. The property also includes restored gardens, a Japanese pond, and Arts & Crafts-inspired gates. Once home to notable families like the Knowltons and Beals, it remains a significant example of 19th-century estate design.

North Hollywood High School is a public high school in North Hollywood, Los Angeles, California, established in 1927. Part of the Los Angeles Unified School District, it serves over 2,500 students and features specialized magnet programs, including the Highly Gifted Magnet, Zoo Magnet, and STEM Magnet. The school offers a variety of Advanced Placement courses, competitive academic teams, and athletics. Known for its Huskies mascot, it has undergone expansions and renovations since its founding. The campus includes multiple academic buildings, sports facilities, and specialized learning spaces.
*Black Noise: Rap Music and Black Culture in Contemporary America* is a 1994 book by Tricia Rose, examining rap music, black culture, and racial politics. It received a 1995 American Book Award.

Jabłonna is a village in Legionowo County, Masovian Voivodeship, Poland, with a population of 7,365. It is known for the historic Jabłonna Palace, a baroque structure built in the late 18th century, and the Mother of God Queen of Poland Church, which was rebuilt after being destroyed in World War II. The village has a rich agricultural history, particularly in greenhouse production during the mid-20th century, and was administratively part of Warsaw from 1975 to 1998.

Akumaa Mama Zimbi, also known as Akumaa, is a Ghanaian actress, radio and television personality, relationship expert, and philanthropist. She gained recognition in the early 1990s as an actress on the Cantata Show. In the early 2000s, she transitioned to radio, eventually hosting the Odo Ahomaso Show on Adom TV, where she offers relationship and marital advice.

Beyond her media career, Akumaa founded Mama Zimbi's Foundation in 2004. The foundation supports underprivileged women and children, particularly widows, and promotes sexual and reproductive health education. She is a recipient of two National Awards and the Radio and Television Personalities Awards.

Zheng Zhi (born 20 August 1980) is a Chinese professional football manager and former player, currently in charge of Guangzhou F.C. A versatile midfielder, he spent most of his career with Guangzhou, captaining the club to multiple Chinese Super League titles and the 2013 AFC Champions League. He was named Asian Footballer of the Year in 2013, becoming only the second Chinese player to win the award.  

Zheng began his career at Liaoning Liaoqing before moving to Shenzhen Jianlibao, where he won his first league title. He later played abroad for Charlton Athletic in England and Celtic in Scotland. Internationally, he earned over 100 caps for China, serving as captain and representing the nation in the 2008 Olympics. After retiring as a player, he transitioned into management, taking over Guangzhou as caretaker before becoming head coach in 2022.

The ZBD-03 is a Chinese airborne infantry fighting vehicle (IFV) designed for the People's Liberation Army. Introduced in 2002, it features a 30mm autocannon, HJ-73C anti-tank missiles, and amphibious capabilities, with variants including command and anti-tank versions.

Lygodium microphyllum, commonly known as Old World climbing fern, is a perennial climbing fern native to tropical Africa, Asia, Australia, and the Pacific Islands. It has become invasive in the southeastern United States and the Caribbean, where it threatens ecosystems by smothering native vegetation and increasing fire risks. Biological control methods, including introduced insects and mites, have been used to manage its spread. The fern has traditional uses in folk medicine and fiber weaving.

Gary McDaid is an Irish Gaelic football manager, referee, and teacher from Glenswilly, County Donegal. He managed Glenswilly to two Donegal Senior Football Championships and served as Donegal's under-20 manager.

Patrick Desmond Callaghan (16 July 1926 – 6 April 1992) was an Air Commodore in the Pakistan Air Force (PAF) and a pioneer officer who served from 1941 to 1971. Initially commissioned into the Royal Indian Air Force, he opted for the PAF after the partition of India. Callaghan played key roles as a flight instructor, combat pilot during the Indo-Pakistani wars of 1965 and 1971, and Chief Inspector of Aviation Safety. After retiring, he became an internationally recognized expert in air crash investigations, assisting several countries.

"Money Jungle" is a studio album by jazz legends Duke Ellington, Charles Mingus, and Max Roach, released in February 1963. Recorded on September 17, 1962, at Sound Makers Studios in New York City, the album features a post-bop style and includes seven tracks, six composed by Ellington and one, "Caravan," by Juan Tizol. The recording session was notable for the generational differences among the musicians, with Ellington being a guiding figure for the younger Mingus and Roach. Despite initial tensions during the session, the album received critical acclaim for its innovative sound and the dynamic interplay between the trio. "Money Jungle" has since been recognized as a significant work in jazz history, influencing countless musicians and earning praise for its artistic merit. The album has been reissued multiple times, with additional tracks and improved sound quality in later releases.

Arti Rana, born around 1976, is a Tharu social entrepreneur known for establishing self-help groups for women in the Terai region of India. She has trained hundreds of women in handicraft production and received several awards, including the Nari Shakti Puraskar in 2022.

The Fine Arts Department (กรมศิลปากร) is a Thai government agency under the Ministry of Culture, established in 1912. It manages archaeological sites, national museums, archives, and cultural heritage.
Michael David Kirby is a former U.S. Ambassador to Serbia and Moldova, serving from 2006 to 2016.

The 2023 BAL qualification, also known as Road to BAL 2023, was a basketball tournament organized by FIBA Africa from October 10 to November 27, 2022. Twenty-one teams participated, competing for slots in the 2023 Basketball Africa League (BAL).
Louis of Brienne (died c. 1297) was the viscount of Beaumont-sur-Sarthe through his marriage to Agnes of Beaumont.

The Philippine Center is an organization established by President Ferdinand Marcos in 1973 to coordinate Philippine government activities in the United States. The first center was established in New York City at 556 Fifth Avenue, in a building purchased from the Knights of Columbus. Subsequent centers were established in San Francisco and Sydney, Australia. The New York center houses government offices and features the Kalayaan Hall auditorium and the Philippine Center Gallery. The building itself was originally designed in 1912 by Carrere & Hastings. The New York center's art collection was exhibited for the first time in 2007.

Clifford B. Harmon (July 1, 1866 – June 25, 1945) was an American real estate developer, amateur aviator, and aviation advocate. He co-founded Wood, Harmon & Co., pioneering installment-based homeownership, and later developed properties in New York and beyond. Harmon was an early aviation enthusiast, setting records in ballooning and airplane flight, including being the first to fly across Long Island Sound in 1910. He established the Harmon Trophy to honor aviation achievements and advocated for international aviation cooperation. Harmon spent his later years in France, where he died in 1945.

*Baaji* (Urdu: باجی) is a 2019 Pakistani social drama film directed by Saqib Malik and Shah Fahad, and written by Irfan Ahmed Urfi. The film stars Meera, Osman Khalid Butt, Amna Ilyas, and Mohsin Abbas Haider. Set against the backdrop of the fading Lollywood film industry, *Baaji* explores themes of ambition, betrayal, and the struggle for relevance.

Produced by Page 33 Films and distributed by ARY Films, *Baaji* was released nationwide on June 28, 2019, and internationally by B4U Films. A teaser trailer was released in April 2019, followed by a theatrical trailer in May. The film generated significant buzz, further amplified by the release of a controversial song.

*Baaji* was a commercial success, grossing Rs. 120 million domestically against a budget of Rs. 60 million. It received generally positive reviews, with critics praising the performances of the cast, particularly Meera and Amna Ilyas. The film was later made available for streaming on Amazon Prime Video and had its television premiere on ARY Digital on Eid al-Fitr, May 26, 2020.

*Vijenac* is a Croatian biweekly magazine focused on literature, arts, and science, published by Matica hrvatska since 1993. It is considered the successor to the 19th-century literary magazine *Vienac*.

The Mizoram Peace Accord was a peace agreement signed between the Mizo National Front (MNF), the Government of India, and the Government of Mizoram in New Delhi on June 30, 1986. It officially ended years of unrest and insurgency in Mizoram.

The conflict stemmed from the incorporation of the Mizo people into British India in the 1870s and subsequent governance under Assam after India's independence in 1947, exacerbated by the Mautam famine in 1959. The MNF, formed in 1961, sought a "Greater Mizoram," leading to armed conflict from 1966. Negotiations, primarily led by church leaders, eventually led to the accord.

Under the agreement, the MNF ceased violent activities. Mizoram was granted statehood in 1987, becoming the 23rd state of India. Laldenga, the leader of the MNF, became the Chief Minister. The accord also led to the establishment of Mizoram University and the declaration of "Remna Ni" (Peace Day) on June 30.

Natalia Maliszewska (born September 16, 1995) is a Polish short track speed skater known for her achievements in the 500 meters, including a silver medal at the 2018 World Championships and a gold medal at the 2019 European Championships.

*The Secret of Roan Inish* is a 1994 independent film directed by John Sayles. Based on Rosalie K. Fry's novel *Secret of the Ron Mor Skerry*, the film tells the story of Fiona (Jeni Courtney), a young girl who is sent to live with her grandparents in a small Irish fishing village in 1946. She hears tales of her family's history, including their evacuation from Roan Inish, their ancestral home, and the legend of a selkie ancestor.

Fiona comes to believe that her baby brother, who was lost at sea, is still alive and being cared for by seals on Roan Inish. With the help of her cousin Eamon, she works to restore the abandoned cottages on the island, hoping to lure her family back and reunite with her brother. The film was shot primarily in County Donegal, Ireland, and was praised for its cinematography and atmosphere.

Fawley is a village and civil parish in West Berkshire, England. It lies on the escarpment of the Berkshire Downs. The village is made up of Great Fawley (or North Fawley) and South Fawley (or Little Fawley). In 2011, the population of the parish was 165. The parish church is dedicated to Saint Mary the Virgin.

A publicist is a professional who manages the public image and media relations of individuals, organizations, or brands. Their primary role is to secure favorable press coverage by crafting press releases, coordinating interviews, and maintaining relationships with journalists, producers, and bloggers. Publicists may work locally, nationally, or internationally, depending on their clients' needs. In the entertainment industry, they help celebrities navigate media exposure, manage crises, and maintain visibility. While some publicists focus on traditional media, others specialize in securing paid media deals for private individuals. The profession requires strong writing skills, networking abilities, and crisis management expertise. Publicists often hold degrees in communications, journalism, or public relations. Their salaries vary, with celebrity publicists typically earning more than corporate or local publicists.

The supine is a grammatical form found in various languages, often derived from a verbal noun and used to express purpose, respect, or other functions. In Latin, there are two supines: the first, ending in *-tum*, is used with verbs of motion or to indicate fate, while the second, ending in *-ū*, is used with adjectives to express respect or extent. Similar forms exist in Sanskrit, Germanic, Finnic, Romance, Slavic, and Baltic languages, with varying uses and functions.
John Mortimer Boudreaux, Jr. (December 10, 1936 – January 14, 2017) was an American rhythm and blues drummer. He is best known for his prolific studio work in New Orleans.

Richard Stöhr (born Richard Stern; 1874 – December 1967) was an Austrian composer and music theorist, known for his contributions to classical music and education. Born in Vienna to Jewish parents, he began composing at the age of six and later studied at the Vienna Academy of Music under Robert Fuchs, where he also became a professor. Stöhr's career flourished in Austria until the rise of the Nazi regime, which led to his dismissal from the Academy due to his Jewish heritage. In 1939, he emigrated to the United States, where he taught at the Curtis Institute of Music and later at Saint Michael's College in Vermont. Throughout his life, Stöhr composed a wide range of works, including symphonies, chamber music, and choral pieces, though many of his later compositions remained unpublished. He passed away in 1967 and is remembered for his adherence to the tonal traditions of the 19th century amidst the evolving musical landscape.

*Satellite in the Sky* is a 1956 British science fiction film directed by Paul Dickson and starring Kieron Moore, Lois Maxwell and Donald Wolfit. The plot concerns the maiden voyage of a British spaceship carrying a nuclear bomb, intended to force global nuclear disarmament. Complications arise when a stowaway reporter and a propulsion malfunction threaten the mission and the crew's lives. It was the first British science fiction film shot in Cinemascope and WarnerColor.
Southfield F.C. was an amateur football club founded in 1881, based in Birmingham, England, and dissolved by 1893.

*Police Academy* is a 1984 American comedy film directed by Hugh Wilson and starring Steve Guttenberg, Kim Cattrall, and Bubba Smith. The film revolves around a new policy requiring the police academy to accept all recruits, leading to a diverse group of individuals joining the force.

The film was a box office success, grossing $149.8 million worldwide against a budget of $4.5 million, becoming the 6th highest grossing American film of 1984. Despite mixed reviews, it spawned a successful franchise of sequels, television series, and animated series.

Exponentiation by squaring is an algorithm for efficiently computing integer powers of a number, or more generally of an element of a semigroup, like a matrix. It involves squaring the base repeatedly and multiplying by the base depending on the binary representation of the exponent. This method significantly reduces the number of multiplications needed compared to a naive approach, especially for large exponents. Variants like the 2k-ary and sliding-window methods further optimize the process. Montgomery's ladder is used in cryptography to protect against side-channel attacks.
William Patrick "Bill" Twomey Sr. (1899–1977) was an Australian rules footballer who played for Collingwood and Hawthorn, and won the 1924 Stawell Gift.

David Peter Ryan (born 5 January 1957) is an English former professional footballer who played as a goalkeeper. He began his career with Manchester United, and later played for Port Vale, Southport, Northwich Victoria, Hyde United and Chorley. He later worked as a coach at Manchester United.

*Great Bliss, Vol. 1* is a 1991 jazz album by saxophonist David S. Ware, recorded with his quartet featuring pianist Matthew Shipp, bassist William Parker, and drummer Marc Edwards. It marked the beginning of Ware's influential quartet and showcased his experimentation with multiple woodwind instruments, including flute and stritch.

Mark V. Campbell (born 1978) is a Canadian musicologist and scholar specializing in Afrosonic innovation, Black Studies, and hip hop archives. He is the author of *Afrosonic Life* (2022), which examines how sonic innovations within the African diaspora articulate methodologies for navigating the afterlife of slavery. Campbell's academic work explores themes of relationality, notions of the human, and the cultural significance of sound.

In 2010, Campbell founded the Northside Hip Hop Archive, dedicated to preserving the history of Canada's hip hop community, particularly its emergence in the 1980s and 1990s. He has curated numerous exhibitions showcasing Canadian hip hop culture, including *Still Tho: Aesthetic Survival in Hip Hop's Visual Art* at the Canada Council for the Arts, *Everything Remains Raw: Photographing Toronto’s Hip Hop Culture from Analogue to Digital* at the McMichael Art Collection, and *For the Record: ‘An Idea of the North’* at the Toronto Reference Library. Other notable exhibitions include *Mixtapes: Hip Hop’s Lost Archive* and the *T-Dot Pioneers* series, which highlight the evolution and legacy of Toronto's hip hop scene. Campbell's work bridges academic inquiry and cultural preservation, emphasizing the importance of hip hop as a site of artistic and historical significance.
*Gutter Phenomenon* is the third studio album by American metalcore band Every Time I Die, released in 2005.
Moritz Schuppert (1817 – May 2, 1887) was a German-born surgeon in New Orleans, known for introducing antisepsis.

Rola Saad (born September 19, 1948) is a Lebanese television producer renowned for her innovative contributions to the media industry in the Middle East. With a portfolio of over 20 productions, including variety shows, reality TV, and documentaries, she has become a media icon. Notably, she directed the popular reality show "Star Academy" and has been involved in other successful programs like "Celebrity Duets" and "Splash."

The Ladies of the House is a 2014 American horror film directed by John Stuart Wildman. The film follows three men who, after a violent altercation with a stripper, are hunted by her cannibalistic roommates. Starring Farah White, Melodie Sisk, Brina Palencia, and Belladonna, it premiered at the Dallas International Film Festival. Critics praised its blend of grindhouse aesthetics with dark humor, with positive reviews from *Dread Central* and *Bloody Disgusting*.

*The Meaning of Life* (also known as *The Meaning of Life with Gay Byrne*) was an Irish interview television series hosted by Gay Byrne. Airing on RTÉ One from 2009 to 2016, the show explored philosophical and religious themes through conversations with notable guests. It ran for 12 series and 50 episodes, drawing an average of 247,000 viewers. Critics noted its increasingly religious tone, with some interviews sparking debate over faith and existential questions.

*Farma* season 12 was the twelfth installment of the Slovak reality television series, airing on Markíza from August 31 to December 11, 2020. Hosted by Eva "Evelyn" Kramerová, the season featured 20 contestants divided into "City" and "Village" teams, competing under contrasting living conditions. Xénia Gregušová emerged as the winner.
Q.S. Serafijn (born in Roosendaal en Nispen) is a Dutch artist and educator known for his interactive sculptures and public art projects.
Air Vice-Marshal Paul Luker CB, OBE, AFC, is a retired Royal Air Force officer. He commanded Joint Helicopter Command before serving in Afghanistan and retiring in 2006.

The 22nd Seanad was the upper house of the Oireachtas (Irish legislature) from 2002 to 2007. It consisted of 60 members: 43 elected from vocational panels, 6 from universities, and 11 nominated by the Taoiseach. Several senators were elected to the Dáil in 2007, leaving vacancies until the Seanad's dissolution.

Bill Long (28 April 1932 – 21 May 2010) was an Irish writer, broadcaster, and lecturer. Known for his radio documentaries and books, including *Bright Light, White Water* and *Change of Heart*, he documented Irish lighthouses and advocated for organ donation following his heart transplant in 1994. Born in Waterford, Long worked as a journalist for several Irish newspapers before transitioning to writing and broadcasting. He also lectured in homiletics at the National University of Ireland, Maynooth, and contributed to RTÉ Radio 1's *Sunday Miscellany*.

Michael Atwell Slater was a British naval officer and surveyor known for his contributions to maritime navigation, including the publication of over 20 Admiralty Charts and improvements to the Caledonian Canal.

Gregg Henry (born May 6, 1952) is an American actor and musician known for his roles in television and film. With a career spanning over four decades, he has appeared in numerous TV series, including *The Riches*, *Scandal*, *The Killing*, and *White Collar*, as well as films such as *Guardians of the Galaxy*. He has also performed in theater, notably playing Julius Caesar in Shakespeare in the Park. Henry is married to theater director Lisa James.

The Simla Conference, held on June 25, 1945, was a pivotal meeting convened by Lord Wavell, the Viceroy of India, to address the political deadlock between the Indian National Congress and the All-India Muslim League. Following the tumultuous Quit India Movement and the subsequent release of Mahatma Gandhi, Wavell sought to create a new Executive Council that would include equal representation for Hindus and Muslims, aiming to foster cooperation among India's diverse political factions. The conference was marked by tensions, particularly between Gandhi and Muhammad Ali Jinnah, as they debated the communal divisions that hindered progress toward Indian self-governance. Despite Wavell's efforts to mediate and propose a plan for constitutional reforms, the conference ultimately failed to achieve its objectives, as disagreements over representation and the future of India persisted. The failure of the Simla Conference set the stage for further political developments leading to Indian independence, as the newly elected Labour government in the UK sought to expedite the transfer of power to Indian leaders.

*L.A. Law: The Movie* (also known as *L.A. Law: Return to Justice*) is a 2002 American drama television film and reunion of the series *L.A. Law*. Directed by Michael Schultz and written by William M. Finkelstein, it aired on NBC on May 12, 2002. Set eight years after the series finale, the film follows the lives of the McKenzie Brackman law firm members as they navigate personal and professional challenges, including legal battles and relationship conflicts.
*Neopleurotomoides rufoapicata* is a species of sea snail, a marine gastropod mollusk in the family Raphitomidae.

Ilias Haddad (born 1 March 1989) is a Dutch-Moroccan professional footballer who plays as a centre-back for Union Touarga. He has previously played for clubs in the Netherlands, Scotland, Bulgaria, and Morocco.

Margaret Bernadine Hall (1863–1910) was a British artist. She studied in Paris in a time when there were few female artists in the city. Hall traveled extensively between 1888 and 1894, and exhibited her paintings in Vienna, Chicago, London and Manchester in 1887. Her painting *Fantine* is held at the Walker Art Gallery.

Joseph Biddle Wilkinson Jr. (February 20, 1845 – October 23, 1915) was an American Confederate Army officer, planter, and federal judge. He served on the Board of General Appraisers from 1890 to 1899, appointed by President Benjamin Harrison. A great-grandson of James Wilkinson, he was also Chief Clerk of the New Orleans Customs House and a Virginia Military Institute graduate.

Tony Waddington (9 November 1924 – 21 January 1994) was an English football player and manager, best known for his 17-year tenure as manager of Stoke City from 1960 to 1977. Born in Manchester, he began his playing career as a wing half with Crewe Alexandra, making 193 league appearances before transitioning into coaching. After joining Stoke City as a coach in 1952, he rose to become manager in 1960, tasked with revitalizing the struggling club.

Waddington is celebrated for transforming Stoke City, notably signing legendary players such as Stanley Matthews, Gordon Banks, and George Eastham. Under his leadership, Stoke achieved promotion to the First Division in 1963 and won their first major trophy, the League Cup, in 1972. His tenure also saw the club reach two FA Cup semi-finals and enjoy one of the most successful periods in its history. However, financial difficulties following storm damage to the Victoria Ground in 1976 led to the sale of key players and Waddington's departure in 1977.

After a brief managerial stint at Crewe Alexandra (1979–1981), Waddington returned to Stoke as an associate director in 1991, a role he held until his death in 1994. He is widely regarded as Stoke City's greatest manager, leaving a lasting legacy in English football.
Bharatpur, West Bengal Assembly constituency is an open constituency in Murshidabad district, currently represented by MLA Humayun Kabir of the All India Trinamool Congress.

"Swing Kids" is a 1993 film directed by Thomas Carter, set in 1939 Hamburg, Germany, that follows a group of teenagers who embrace American swing music amidst the rise of the Nazi regime. The film explores themes of friendship, resistance, and the impact of totalitarianism on youth culture. Despite mixed reviews, it highlights the historical Swingjugend movement.

In Israel, a local council is a type of municipal government for settlements that meet a minimum population threshold, typically over 2,000 residents, but are not large enough to be designated as cities. The Israeli Interior Minister determines a locality's status, considering residents' preferences. Local councils play a key role in town planning and are represented nationally by the Union of Local Authorities in Israel, established in 1938.

"The Moral Obligation to Be Intelligent" is an essay by John Erskine, published in 1915, that explores the philosophical imperative of intellectual engagement. The essay was later included in the 1963 collection "Gateway to the Great Books" and was featured in a posthumous compilation of selected essays, also titled "The Moral Obligation to Be Intelligent," published in 2000. This edition was edited by Lionel Trilling, a former student of Erskine, and includes an introduction by Leon Wieseltier, highlighting the essay's significance in the context of Erskine's broader philosophical contributions.

The 2017 Big 12 Conference football season was part of the 2017 NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision season. The season began on September 2, 2017, and concluded on December 2, 2017. Ten teams competed in the Big 12 Conference. Television partners included ABC, FOX, ESPN, FS1, FSN, and LHN.

Oklahoma was selected as the preseason favorite to win the conference. The season culminated in the Big 12 Championship Game, with Oklahoma winning the conference.

Following the season, Oklahoma quarterback Baker Mayfield was the first player from the Big 12 Conference to be selected in the 2018 NFL Draft. He was drafted first overall by the Cleveland Browns. Throughout the season, the Big 12 Conference recognized players of the week. Several Big 12 players were also named to the 2017 College Football All-America Team.

Coupon Mountain was a website that provided coupon codes and printable coupons to online shoppers. Founded in 2001 by Harry Tsao and Talmadge O'Neill, it operated through affiliate marketing partnerships with retailers such as Target, Walmart, and Nordstrom. Its model aggregated coupons from various merchants, similar to a newspaper circular.

In addition to its U.S. site, Coupon Mountain maintained international versions in the United Kingdom, Germany, and China. The website's services were free and did not require registration, offering email notifications, a weekly newsletter, and RSS feeds. It featured 16 categories of products.

Coupon Mountain was recognized as a popular online coupon source by *The Wall Street Journal* and *The Washington Post* in 2008, and was also featured on CNN, MSNBC, and the *Chicago Tribune*. The website ceased operations in October 2014. It was formerly a division of MeziMedia (2001-2010), ValueClick Brands (2010-2013), and IAC (2013-2014).

Sir Frederick Mann KCMG (2 May 1869 – 29 May 1958) was an Australian judge who served as Chief Justice of Victoria from 1935 to 1944. He was also the Lieutenant Governor of Victoria from 1936 to 1945.

Born in South Australia, Mann studied law at the University of Melbourne while working for the Victorian Government. He served in the Boer War and later specialized as a barrister in common law and equity. He was appointed to the Supreme Court of Victoria in 1919 and was knighted in 1933.

*Primitive Love* is a studio album by Miami Sound Machine, released on August 13, 1985, by Epic Records and CBS Discos International. It was produced by Emilio Estefan Jr. The album followed up on the band's previous releases in its music, rhythms, and Gloria Estefan's vocals. *Primitive Love* marked the band's first appearance on the American albums chart, reaching number 23 on the Billboard 200. Three singles from the album reached the Top 10 of the Billboard Hot 100: "Conga", "Bad Boy", and "Words Get in the Way".

Tamara Bowie (born June 3, 1981) is an American former professional basketball player and college standout. She played for the Ball State Cardinals from 1999 to 2003, becoming the program's all-time leading scorer and a two-time Mid-American Conference Player of the Year. Professionally, she played internationally in Iceland, Israel, and Bulgaria, earning accolades such as Úrvalsdeild Foreign Player of the Year and leading Grindavík in scoring and rebounds. Bowie also helped WBC Neftokhimik Burgas win the Bulgarian League championship in 2011.

Monophysitism is a Christological doctrine asserting that Jesus Christ has only one divine nature, rather than two distinct natures (divine and human) as upheld by the Council of Chalcedon (451). Emerging in the 5th century, it opposed Dyophysitism and was associated with Miaphysite churches, though many later distanced themselves from extreme interpretations. Monophysitism influenced theological and political conflicts in the Byzantine Empire, leading to schisms and imperial attempts at compromise.

"Stay" is a 1990 R&B single by Glenn Jones from his album *All for You*, written and produced by Mark Stevens and Bobby Khozouri. In 1993, British girl group Eternal released a successful cover as their debut single, reaching number four on the UK Singles Chart and number 19 on the US Billboard Hot 100.

"Iced Out Audemars" is a drill song by American rapper Pop Smoke featuring Dafi Woo, released on July 20, 2020, as a track on the deluxe version of Pop Smoke's posthumous debut album, *Shoot for the Stars, Aim for the Moon*. The song was written by Pop Smoke, Dafi Woo, L3gion, and Rico Beats, with production handled solely by Rico Beats and additional production by L3gion.

A remix of the song featuring Lil Wayne surfaced online in October 2020 and was released later that month. The remix was met with generally positive reviews, with critics praising Lil Wayne's energy, bars, and tribute to Pop Smoke, who had been murdered earlier that year. Lil Wayne's verse includes a melodic approach that shifts to a rapid-fire flow, with lyrics referencing Pop Smoke and Wu-Tang Clan.
"Radar's Report" is the third episode of M*A*S*H's second season, featuring Radar's voice-over as he types a report amidst comedic hospital events.

Goodwin Park is a 237-acre (96 ha) urban park in Hartford and Wethersfield, Connecticut. Established in 1901, it was designed by Frederick Law Olmsted and originally featured a large meadow, later converted into the Goodwin Park Golf Course, which includes a historic nine-hole course dating to the late 1800s. The park was named in honor of Reverend Francis Goodwin, a key advocate for Hartford's park system. It also briefly served as an early airmail landing strip.

Ma Zhou (601–648) was a prominent politician during the Tang dynasty, known for his insightful counsel to Emperor Taizong. Born into a poor family in Shandong, he initially struggled in his early career as a teacher before gaining recognition for his suggestions to the emperor, which emphasized frugality and effective governance. Ma served in various high-ranking positions, including as the acting head of the legislative bureau, and was known for his logical thinking and historical analysis. He died in 648 and was posthumously honored as the Duke of Gaotang.

Michael Rowan "Mick" O'Driscoll (born 8 October 1978) is an Irish former rugby union player. A lock, he played for Munster and USA Perpignan, winning 23 caps for Ireland. He was a member of Ireland's 2009 Grand Slam winning team.

*Lunella undulata* is a marine snail species endemic to Australia, found in coastal waters from New South Wales to Western Australia. It is notable for its bright green, umbilicate shell and presence in archaeological middens.

Peter Friedrich Röding (17 June 1767 – 8 June 1846) was a German malacologist best known for his 1798 work *Museum Boltenianum*, a catalog of mollusk specimens from the collection of Joachim Friedrich Bolten. Though initially overlooked, the catalog introduced many valid taxonomic names, later recognized by William Healey Dall. The International Commission on Zoological Nomenclature (ICZN) ruled in 1956 that Röding, not Bolten, authored the taxonomic descriptions in the work.
Ashley Clements is an American actress best known for starring as Lizzie Bennet in the Emmy-winning web series *The Lizzie Bennet Diaries*.

Norma Aleandro (born May 2, 1936) is an acclaimed Argentine actress known for her significant contributions to film and theater. Born in Buenos Aires to actor parents, she began her career in 1952 and gained international recognition with her role in the 1985 film *The Official Story*, which won an Academy Award and earned her the Cannes Award for Best Actress. Aleandro has appeared in several notable Argentine films, including *Son of the Bride* and *Sol de Otoño*, as well as Hollywood productions like *Gaby: A True Story*, for which she received an Academy Award nomination. Throughout her career, she has collaborated frequently with actor Héctor Alterio. In addition to her film work, Aleandro has been honored as an "Illustrious Citizen" of Buenos Aires and has returned to the stage, winning the María Guerrero award in 1996.

The Catskill Mountain House was a hotel built in 1824 on a plateau in the Catskill Mountains. It became a popular subject for artists and writers, including those of the Hudson River School. The hotel declined in popularity and was burned down by the State of New York in 1963. Today, the site offers sweeping views and is near the North-South Lake campground.

Patillas is a municipality located on the southeastern coast of Puerto Rico, known as "La Esmeralda del Sur" for its lush landscapes. Founded in 1811, it features 16 barrios and has a population of approximately 15,985 as of 2020. The town is characterized by its beaches, agricultural production, and historical significance, including the impact of Hurricane Maria in 2017. Patillas is governed by Mayor Maritza Sánchez Neris and is part of the Humacao senatorial district.

Billy J. Kramer (born William Howard Ashton on August 19, 1943) is an English pop singer known for his contributions to the Merseybeat genre during the 1960s. He gained fame with hits such as "Do You Want to Know a Secret?" and "Little Children," both of which were penned by John Lennon and Paul McCartney. Kramer's collaboration with the Dakotas, his backing band, led to several chart successes, including "Bad to Me" and "I'll Keep You Satisfied." After a decline in popularity, he continued to perform and record, releasing new music into the 21st century and participating in various tours and projects.
Doon Valley is an electoral ward in East Ayrshire, Scotland, established in 2007. It includes Dalmellington, Drongan, and Patna, with three councillors representing the area.

The spectacle case pearly mussel (Cumberlandia monodonta) is an endangered freshwater bivalve native to the Mississippi, Ohio, and Missouri River basins. Characterized by its oblong shell, which can grow over 9 inches, this species exhibits a color change from light yellowish-green in youth to brown or black in maturity. The life cycle of C. monodonta involves a parasitic larval stage on fish, with specific reproductive details still largely unknown. Populations have significantly declined due to habitat loss, pollution, and competition from invasive species like zebra mussels. Conservation efforts are ongoing, but the species faces severe threats and has a low recovery potential, with no finalized recovery plan in place.

In mathematics, the Tor functor is a right exact functor that arises in the context of module theory, specifically for left R-modules over a ring R. It is defined for a fixed left R-module B and takes right R-modules A to abelian groups. The Tor groups, denoted as Tor_i(A, B), are derived from the left derived functors of the functor G(B) = A ⊗_R B, where A is tensor-multiplied with a projective resolution of B. These groups can also be computed using homology from a projective resolution of A. For commutative rings, Tor groups yield R-modules, while for non-commutative rings, they are generally only abelian groups. The functor exhibits properties that are crucial for computations in homological algebra.

Vincent is a census-designated place (CDP) in Los Angeles County, California, with a population of 15,922 as of the 2010 census. Located at an elevation of 3,225 feet (983 m), it covers 1.5 square miles (3.9 km²) entirely on land. The community is predominantly Hispanic or Latino (74.9%), with a median age of 32.4 years. Politically, it lies within California's 32nd congressional district.

Pheona Wall is a Ugandan lawyer and student leader. She served as president of the Uganda Law Society (2020–present) and held leadership roles at the National Water and Sewerage Corporation. Known for her advocacy work, she has received awards for justice and legal innovation.

The Pearl Carpet of Baroda is a 19th-century treasure adorned with pearls, diamonds, rubies, and emeralds, originally commissioned by Maharaja Khande Rao Gaekwad of Baroda in 1865. Intended as a gift for the Prophet's Tomb in Medina, the carpet remained with the Gaekwad family after the maharaja's death in 1870. It was first publicly displayed at the 1903 Delhi Durbar and later owned by Sita Devi, who took it to Monaco in 1946. The carpet reappeared in 1985 at the Metropolitan Museum in New York and was sold at a Sotheby’s auction in Doha in 2009 for $5.5 million. It is now housed in the National Museum of Qatar. Measuring 264 by 173 centimeters, the carpet is made of deerskin and silk, showcasing intricate craftsmanship with its lavish embellishments.
John Duncan (1794–1881) was a British botanist known for his extensive herbarium and contributions to botany.

Lord Frederick de Vere Beauclerk (1773–1850) was an English clergyman and cricketer known for his controversial presence in 19th-century cricket. A right-handed all-rounder, he played for the Marylebone Cricket Club from 1791 to 1825 and was noted for his competitive nature and tactical acumen. Despite his ecclesiastical role, he was infamous for his gambling and contentious behavior on and off the field. Beauclerk was the fourth son of the 5th Duke of St Albans and married Charlotte Dillon-Lee, with whom he had four children.

A limiting factor is a variable that restricts the growth, abundance, or distribution of a system, organism, or process. The concept is used across disciplines, including ecology, business, technology, and chemistry. In ecology, it is based on Liebig's Law of the Minimum, where growth is controlled by the scarcest resource. Limiting factors can be physical, biological, or circumstantial, and may lead to competition. In chemistry, they determine reaction yields and rates.

In computer vision, a dynamic texture is an image sequence that exhibits certain temporal stationary properties.

Methods for analyzing dynamic textures are used in a variety of applications, including: segmenting sequence images of natural scenes, which can be used in navigation applications; detecting motion, such as abnormal crowd activities from footage videos; classifying videos of natural scenes or other scenes that exhibit dynamic textures; and retrieving videos that contain dynamic textures such as sea waves, smoke, clouds, or wavy trees.
Michael Atwell Slater (died February 2, 1842) was a British Royal Navy officer and hydrographer. Promoted to Commander in 1837, he contributed to Admiralty Charts and coastal surveys of Britain.
Markku Peltoniemi (born 8 February 1948) is a Finnish former footballer who played as a midfielder for HJK Helsinki and HPS Helsinki.

Fredrik Backman (born June 2, 1981) is a Swedish columnist, blogger, and novelist. He debuted as a novelist in 2012 with *A Man Called Ove*, which was adapted into a film. Many of his books have been translated into English. Backman has written for *Helsingborgs Dagblad* and *Moore Magazine*. He is married to Neda Shafti Backman, and his book *Things My Son Needs to Know about the World* was based on his experiences with parenting.

Michelle Stephenson (born 3 January 1977) is an English singer and television presenter, best known as an original member of the girl group Touch, which later became the Spice Girls. She left the group in 1994 after a brief tenure, citing personal reasons, including her mother's illness, though other accounts suggest differing explanations. Following her departure, Stephenson pursued a career in television presenting and music, working as a session singer and releasing an EP titled *SHESongs* in 2003.

"Baankey Ki Crazy Baraat" is a 2015 Indian Hindi comedy film directed by Aijaz Khan, featuring Rajpal Yadav, Sanjay Mishra, and Tia Bajpai. The story follows Baankey, a 35-year-old bachelor facing marriage obstacles due to a horoscope issue. To resolve this, his family arranges a proxy wedding, leading to unexpected romantic entanglements and comedic situations.
**Fred Crawford** (1910–1974) was an All-American college football player for Duke University and briefly played professionally.

Kurihara (栗原市) is a city in Miyagi Prefecture, Japan, known for its agricultural landscape, hot springs, and scenic beauty around Mount Kurikoma. Established in 2005 through the merger of several towns and villages, it covers 804.97 km² and had a population of 66,565 as of 2020. The economy is primarily based on rice farming, with historical ties to mining. The city features attractions like Lake Izunuma's lotus flowers and seasonal foliage.

Shigeichi Nagano (1925–2019) was a Japanese photographer known for his street photography, particularly his humorous depictions of Tokyo's salarymen during Japan's economic boom of the 1960s. He also worked in film and television after working on Tokyo Olympiad.

USCGC Robert Yered (WPC-1104) is a Sentinel-class cutter of the United States Coast Guard, launched on March 2, 2012, and commissioned on February 15, 2013. Named after Engineman First Class Robert J. Yered, the vessel is designed for coastal security, drug interdiction, and search and rescue operations, and is currently in active service.
Bradley Francis Granger (1825–1882) was a Republican politician and lawyer who served as a U.S. Representative from Michigan's 1st district from 1861 to 1863.

Larissa Takchi (born 1997) is an Australian television cook of Lebanese origin. She won *MasterChef Australia* in 2019. Her winning dish was "Szechuan Pavlova". Prior to *MasterChef*, she managed her mother's cafe.

Norman Louis Haggett (8 July 1926 – 22 February 2018) was an English cricketer, Royal Air Force officer, and teacher. A right-handed batsman, he played four first-class matches for the Combined Services team between 1962 and 1964. He served in the RAF for 25 years, becoming a parachute instructor and testing new parachute designs, later receiving an MBE in 1981. After retiring, he taught mathematics and cricket at Radley College. A former chairman of the West Oxfordshire Conservative Party, he died in Oxford at age 91.

Navajo music encompasses a rich tradition of vocal and instrumental compositions, deeply rooted in the cultural and spiritual practices of the Navajo people. Traditional music includes sacred ceremonial songs, used to restore harmony and balance, as well as "popular" songs for public performance. Other forms include children's songs, peyote songs associated with the Native American Church, and the Shoegame, a traditional winter activity. Contemporary Navajo music spans diverse genres, including punk, metal, hip-hop, and country, blending traditional elements with modern influences.

Joseph Lawson Howze (August 30, 1923 – January 9, 2019) was an American prelate of the Catholic Church and the first Black bishop to lead a U.S. diocese. He served as the first bishop of the Diocese of Biloxi from 1977 to 2001, following earlier roles as a priest and auxiliary bishop.

Basharat Mirbabayeva (5 December 1916 – 20 March 2010) was an Uzbek railway worker and the first Uzbek woman to parachute from an airplane. She became a lead locomotive driver in 1939, organized a women’s locomotive brigade during World War II, and later promoted youth education.

The *New York World* was a major American newspaper founded in 1860 and published until 1931. Under Joseph Pulitzer’s ownership (1883–1911), it became a leader in investigative journalism and sensational "yellow journalism," popularizing comic strips and pioneering color printing. Notable for its exposés, including Nellie Bly’s undercover reporting, the *World* also introduced the first crossword puzzle and published *The World Almanac*. After Pulitzer’s death, his sons ran the paper until its sale to Scripps-Howard, which shut it down in 1931. A digital revival, *The New York World*, launched in 2011 but ceased updates by 2016.

Chenani is a town in the Udhampur district of Jammu and Kashmir, India, located at an elevation of 1,062 meters. Historically ruled by a Rajput clan until 1947, it has a population of 2,620 as of the 2011 Census, with a literacy rate of 88.91%. Chenani serves as a gateway to several religious shrines and is well-connected by road, including the Jammu–Srinagar National Highway.

Entrenching battalions were temporary units formed during World War I by the British, Canadian, and New Zealand armies to perform labor-intensive tasks such as improving defenses and serving as reserve forces. The British Army established them in 1916, disbanding them in 1918 after the German spring offensive. The Canadian Expeditionary Force created similar units in 1916, disbanding them in 1917, while New Zealand formed entrenching battalions in 1918, disbanding them later that year.

"In Melbourne Tonight" was an Australian variety television show hosted by Graham Kennedy, airing from 1957 to 1970 and briefly revived from 1996 to 1998. The program featured a mix of comedy, music, and interviews, becoming a significant part of Australian television history. It showcased numerous presenters and established a legacy of live variety programming, with Kennedy's charisma being a key factor in its success.

GAL (born 1940) is a Belgian cartoonist known for his socially engaged and politically charged artwork. He began publishing cartoons in 1960 and has contributed to various magazines, including Knack. GAL's unique style resembles painting, and he has illustrated numerous books and political posters, notably against apartheid.

The mountain ground squirrel (Geosciurus princeps) is a rodent found in the southwest arid region of Africa, from southern Angola to southern Namibia and as far south as Richtersveld National Park. It is classified as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List.

This large-bodied squirrel has small ears, with a head and body length of 23 to 29 cm, a tail length of 21 to 28 cm, and a weight of 490 to 710 grams. Its body is covered in short, pale cinnamon brown hair, with white fur on its belly, around the eyes, and on the front of its face. A white stripe extends from its shoulders to its hips. Mountain ground squirrels are diurnal, with females living alone or in small family groups, while males are mostly solitary.
*Madonna and Child Kissing* is an oil-on-panel painting by Quentin Matsys, created circa 1525–1530. Depicting Mary and Jesus in a richly decorated setting, it is housed at the Mauritshuis, The Hague.
Reliance was an American automobile manufacturer (1904–1909) known for its early side-entrance tonneau cars and later commercial trucks, acquired by General Motors.

Rajmohan's Wife (1864) is an English-language novella by Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay. Set in rural Bengal, it follows Matangini, a young woman trapped in an abusive marriage to Rajmohan, who becomes entangled in a plot involving theft, betrayal, and forbidden love. The story explores themes of female agency, social constraints, and moral dilemmas in 19th-century India. It is considered one of the earliest Indian novels written in English.

Gorky Park, also known as Gorodskoy Garden, is a historic urban park located in Rostov-on-Don, Russia. Established in 1813 on the site of a former town dump, the park spans 12.2 hectares (30 acres) and features landscaped gardens, walking paths, fountains, sculptures, and recreational facilities. Initially formed by combining several smaller gardens, the park was developed with contributions from private organizations and underwent significant improvements over the years, including the addition of exotic plants, a stone grotto, and a reservoir with swans.

Notable landmarks include the first Municipal Theatre, a rotunda, and the park's iconic entrance designed by architect Nikolai Doroshenko. Today, it remains a popular cultural and recreational destination.

The Parapetí River is a 500 km (310 mi) long river in Bolivia, originating in the Cordillera Central and flowing into the Bañados del Izozog wetlands in the Gran Chaco region. These wetlands, primarily fed by the river, are internationally protected under the Ramsar Convention and partially included in the Kaa-Iya del Gran Chaco National Park.

Radio Dabanga is a Sudanese media outlet that provides independent news and information, primarily focusing on conflict areas such as Darfur. Founded on December 1, 2008, by a coalition of Sudanese journalists and international NGOs, it is operated by the Dutch NGO Free Press Unlimited. Initially known as Radio Darfur, it broadcasts in Sudanese Arabic and has expanded its programming to include various shows, such as "Lost and Found" and "Hababkum Darfur," which engage listeners and address community issues. Radio Dabanga's broadcasts reach approximately 2.3 million listeners daily via shortwave radio and an average of 1 million viewers through its satellite TV channel, which began in 2018. Despite facing censorship and attempts by the Sudanese government to silence its broadcasts, Radio Dabanga has maintained a significant presence, reporting from exile in Amsterdam and utilizing audience participation to gather news. The station has adapted its coverage to include a broader range of topics relevant to all Sudanese citizens.
The 1945–46 season saw Scottish football continue in a wartime format, with regional leagues and unofficial internationals. Rangers won the Southern League A Division.

Prospect Hill is a historic neighborhood in Omaha, Nebraska, known for its cemetery and public housing developments. Established in the 1850s during the Scriptown affair, the area became home to Prospect Hill Cemetery, with its first official burial in 1858. The cemetery includes the graves of over 800 African Americans, including at least 10 Buffalo Soldiers. In the 1940s, the U.S. government constructed nearly 700 public housing units in the neighborhood, including Hilltop Homes, Spencer Homes, and Pleasantview Homes. Today, Salem Baptist Church occupies the former site of Hilltop Homes, reflecting the neighborhood's evolving history and significance.

Sylvia Fine (August 29, 1913 – October 28, 1991) was an American composer and producer, best known for her work with her husband, actor Danny Kaye. Fine wrote many of Kaye's songs and played a significant role in managing his career. She received a Peabody Award and was nominated for two Oscars and two Emmys. Later in life, she taught musical comedy and produced television programs.

Henry Stuart Russell (born in Halliford, Middlesex, England, 1818 – died March 5, 1889) was an explorer and member of the Legislative Council of New South Wales. He was the first European to discover the Boyne River.

Lau is a socken and district located on the east coast of Gotland, Sweden, encompassing a small village surrounding the medieval Lau Church. Covering an area of 28 km², Lau has a population of 227 as of 2014. The region features rural landscapes, including farms and forests, and is home to notable sites such as the Lau Käldu spring and the Lau Islets. Historically, Lau dates back to medieval times and has significant archaeological finds, including grave mounds and a 12th-century defense tower.

The University of Florida (UF) offers a distance education program in clinical toxicology, including a Master of Science (MS) degree and a graduate certificate. The programs, overseen by Dr. Ian Tebbett, are designed for health science professionals seeking advanced knowledge in toxicology. The MS program requires 32 credits, while the graduate certificate consists of 15 online credits. The certificate provides a background in the medical and toxicological principles of toxicants commonly encountered in poison control centers and emergency departments. A bachelor's degree in a health, health related or science related field is required before prospective students can enter the certificate program.

*The Complete Beat* is a box set by the British ska and new wave band The English Beat, released on July 10, 2012, by Shout! Factory. Spanning recordings from 1979 to 1982, the collection includes the band’s three studio albums, extended mixes, dub versions, live recordings, and a 24-page booklet by Alex Ogg detailing the band’s history. While no previously unreleased songs are included, many rarities make their CD debut in this set.

The box set received critical acclaim, earning a score of 90/100 on Metacritic based on eight reviews. Critics praised its comprehensive nature and the quality of the live recordings, which showcase the band’s energetic performances and political themes. AllMusic and Rolling Stone both rated it 4.5/5, with reviewers highlighting the visceral force of the recordings and the band’s enduring impact. Paste magazine also lauded the live tracks, calling them the most compelling aspect of the collection.

VirusTotal is an online service launched in June 2004 that analyzes files and URLs for security threats using multiple antivirus engines and tools. Headquartered in Dublin, Ireland, it is available in numerous languages and serves users worldwide. Originally created by Hispasec Sistemas, VirusTotal was acquired by Google LLC in 2012 and is currently operated by Chronicle, a subsidiary of Alphabet Inc. 

The platform allows users to upload files (up to 128 MB) or submit URLs for scanning, providing detailed reports on potential threats. It offers various applications, including a Windows Uploader, Mac OS X app, and browser extensions, facilitating easy access to its scanning capabilities. Additionally, VirusTotal provides a public API for automation, enabling users to integrate its features into their own applications. While the service is free, uploaded files may be shared with anti-malware companies and retained for analysis. VirusTotal is widely used by security professionals and individuals seeking to identify and mitigate potential malware risks.

A lord proprietor was an individual granted extensive powers over a proprietary colony, typically by a monarch, allowing them to govern, create laws, and appoint officials. This system became prominent in the British colonial era, particularly following Maryland's Royal Grant in 1632.

John William Goode Jr. (March 10, 1923 – February 5, 1994) was an American lawyer and Republican politician from San Antonio, Texas. A World War II veteran, he served as a Marine Corps captain and received the Silver Star and Bronze Star for his bravery. After earning his law degree, Goode held various legal positions, including acting first assistant district attorney, and was involved in Republican politics, notably running for Congress in 1961. He was inducted into the Bexar County Republican Party's Hall of Fame shortly before his death.

*Fear* is the third studio album by Toad the Wet Sprocket, released on August 27, 1991, by Columbia Records. The album was recorded at Granny's House in Reno, Nevada, and produced, recorded, and mixed by Gavin MacKillop, who would also produce their next two albums.

Columbia Records financed the mixing of fourteen songs, twelve of which were chosen for release on the album. The album spawned the singles "Is It for Me", "All I Want", "Hold Her Down", "Walk on the Ocean", and "I Will Not Take These Things for Granted".

Natalia B. Shustova is a prominent chemist and professor at the University of South Carolina, known for her research in metal-organic frameworks and their applications in sustainable energy, sensors, and nuclear waste management. She holds dual Ph.D. degrees and has received numerous awards for her contributions to the field.

The Professor, whose full name is Roy Hinkley, is a fictional character from the television series *Gilligan's Island*, portrayed by Russell Johnson. A high school science teacher and botanist, he joined the ill-fated voyage to write a book titled *Fun with Ferns*. Known for his ingenuity, he created numerous inventions from coconuts and bamboo to aid the castaways, though he was unable to repair their boat. The Professor was depicted as intelligent, level-headed, and asexual, often serving as a mediator among the group. His character has been referenced and parodied in popular culture, highlighting his resourcefulness and the irony of his inability to escape the island.
Ole Herman Johannes Krag (1837–1916) was a Norwegian military officer and firearms designer best known for co-designing the Krag–Jørgensen rifle.
The 62nd Ohio Infantry Regiment was a Union Army unit active from 1861 to 1865, fighting in battles such as Fort Wagner and the Siege of Petersburg.

Nettle is a low-level cryptographic library written in C, developed by Niels Möller, and licensed under LGPLv3 and GPLv2/3. It provides a wide range of cryptographic algorithms, including block ciphers, stream ciphers, digests, and public-key algorithms. Designed for flexibility and simplicity, Nettle avoids application-specific features like algorithm selection or memory allocation, serving as a core library for building cryptographic applications. It is used by GnuTLS and supports platforms like x86 and x86-64 with assembly optimizations.

"All Good Things..." is the series finale of the MTV reality television series *The Hills*, which aired on July 13, 2010. The episode follows Kristin Cavallari as she prepares to move to Europe, while her friends—including Brody Jenner, Audrina Patridge, and Lo Bosworth—navigate their own personal transitions. The finale concludes with a meta twist, revealing that the show's final scene was filmed on a backlot, breaking the illusion of reality.  

An alternate ending, aired in August 2013, featured original star Lauren Conrad reuniting with Jenner, providing closure for longtime fans. The episode received mixed reviews, with critics divided over its self-referential ending and the alternate version. The finale drew 3 million viewers, marking the season's highest ratings.

The National Trust for Scotland is a conservation charity founded in 1931 to protect Scotland's natural and cultural heritage. It owns and manages over 130 properties, including castles, gardens, and 180,000 acres of countryside, such as Glen Coe and St Kilda. With around 330,000 members, it operates similarly to the National Trust in England, Wales, and Northern Ireland. The Trust relies on membership fees, donations, and volunteers to maintain its sites.

Sir John Wallace Pringle (1863–1938) was a British military engineer and railway inspector. He served in the Third Anglo-Burmese War and led surveys for the Uganda Railway. As Chief Inspecting Officer of Railways for the Board of Trade and later the Ministry of Transport, he investigated accidents and promoted railway safety. Knighted in 1925, he retired in 1929.
Margit L. McCorkle was an American-Canadian musicologist known for her thematic catalogues of Johannes Brahms and Robert Schumann.

Fight Sports is a Brazilian jiu-jitsu (BJJ) academy founded in Miami in 2011 by Roberto "Cyborg" Abreu. By 2022, Fight Sports had over thirty affiliated schools worldwide. In 2021, Fight Sports partnered with Melqui Galvao's project in Brazil. In 2018, Fight Sports faced controversy when one of its coaches was arrested and charged with sexual assault.

John Gillespie Hill Jr. (August 9, 1926 – March 28, 1999) was a United States Army major general who served from 1946 to 1978, participating in the Korean and Vietnam Wars.
