He was awarded the Distinguished Service Cross for valor during the Korean War and held key leadership roles in Vietnam, including commanding the 1st Brigade, 5th Mechanized Infantry Division, and serving as II Corps deputy senior adviser during the Battle of Kontum. After retiring, he became a lawyer in Austin, Texas. Hill died in 1999 from brain cancer and is buried at Arlington National Cemetery.

In United States tax law, an offer in compromise (OIC) is an agreement between a taxpayer and the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) that settles the taxpayer's tax liabilities for less than the full amount owed. To qualify, taxpayers must meet specific conditions, such as doubt as to liability or collectibility, or demonstrate that full payment would cause economic hardship. The IRS assesses the taxpayer's ability to pay, income, expenses, and asset equity. The IRS requires a partial payment with the OIC, but low-income taxpayers may be exempt. An OIC stops tax levies, but tax liens remain until the offer is accepted and paid.
William Horsell (1807–1863) was a British hydrotherapist, publisher, temperance and vegetarianism activist. He founded the Nature's Beverage Society and operated a vegetarian hospital.
Sangita Patel (born January 2, 1979) is a Canadian television personality, known for her work on *Entertainment Tonight Canada*, *CP24*, and *The Weather Network*.
Tateyama Station (館山駅) is a railway station in Tateyama, Chiba, Japan, operated by JR East. It serves the Uchibō Line and has one side platform and one island platform.

Franciszek Piłsudski (1713–1791) was a Polish-Lithuanian nobleman, Colonel, and Deputy Cup Bearer. He served in the Grand Ducal Lithuanian army and was a member of the Sejm. Piłsudski expanded his family's estates and was married twice, having two children. He received notable awards, including the Order of Saint Stanislaus.
Gretta Kehoe-Quigley is a former Irish camogie player from Wexford, who played as a centre back for the Wexford senior team from 1969 to 1975, winning a Leinster senior championship medal in 1974.

Josef Kiefel (2 October 1909 – 1988) was a German communist and intelligence officer. After fleeing Nazi Germany for the Soviet Union, he fought in World War II as a partisan operative. Postwar, he helped establish East Germany's security apparatus and led the Stasi's counterintelligence department from 1953 to 1960.

The 1973 World Snooker Championship was a professional snooker tournament held from 16 to 28 April 1973 at the City Exhibition Halls in Manchester, England. Sponsored by Park Drive, it featured a record 24 entrants and a £8,000 prize fund, with £1,500 awarded to the winner. Defending champion Alex Higgins was eliminated in the semi-finals by Eddie Charlton, while Ray Reardon staged a dramatic comeback from 12–19 down to defeat John Spencer 23–22 in the other semi-final.  

In the best-of-75 frames final, Reardon overcame Charlton 38–32 to claim his second world title, having previously won in 1970. The tournament marked a shift in snooker's popularity, with 25,000 spectators attending and partial BBC television coverage of the final. Reardon's victory was notable for his recovery from a 0–7 deficit in the opening session. The highest break of the event was 139, made by Spencer in the second round.

Robert Simpson Neighbors (November 3, 1815 – September 14, 1859) was an American soldier and Indian agent known for his efforts to protect Native American tribes in Texas, particularly the Comanche. After immigrating to Texas in 1836, he served in the Army of the Republic of Texas and later became an influential Indian agent, implementing the innovative "field system" to engage directly with tribes. Neighbors was assassinated in 1859, likely due to his advocacy for Native American rights.

Henry Hastings, 3rd Earl of Huntingdon (1535 – 14 December 1595) was an English nobleman, administrator, and military commander. A descendant of the Plantagenets, he was educated alongside Edward VI and later married Katherine Dudley, daughter of the Duke of Northumberland. His support for Lady Jane Grey’s claim to the throne briefly led to his imprisonment under Mary I. Under Elizabeth I, he became a trusted Protestant loyalist, serving as President of the Council of the North (1572–1595) to counter Catholic threats, including Mary, Queen of Scots. Despite his royal lineage, his Yorkist ancestry hindered his political ambitions. He died in York, heavily in debt, and was buried at St Helen’s Church, Ashby-de-la-Zouch.

Law & Order: Special Victims Unit (season 8) premiered on NBC on September 19, 2006, and concluded on May 22, 2007, with 22 episodes. The season starred Christopher Meloni, Mariska Hargitay, Richard Belzer, Ice-T, Diane Neal, BD Wong, Tamara Tunie, and Dann Florek. Notable guest stars included Connie Nielsen, Chris "Ludacris" Bridges, Leslie Caron (who won an Emmy), and Jerry Lewis. The season introduced Adam Beach as Detective Chester Lake and featured storylines involving Olivia Benson's half-brother (played by Michael Weston).

Boon Island is a small island in the Gulf of Maine, located off the coast of Maine, United States. It is known for the Boon Island Light, the tallest lighthouse in New England, built in 1855. The island's name is believed to originate from the 1682 wreck of the Increase, where survivors considered their rescue a "boon." Other shipwrecks include the Nottingham Galley in 1710, infamous for cannibalism among the survivors, and the Empire Knight in 1944, which released mercury into the sea. The lighthouse is now owned by Boon Island LLC.

Laura Natalia Esquivel (born May 18, 1994) is an Argentine actress and singer known for her role as the lead in the popular teen telenovela *Patito Feo* (2007–2008). She gained early recognition on the reality show *Código F.A.M.A.* and has since achieved international success, particularly in Latin America and Europe, with sold-out concerts, award-winning soundtracks, and roles in Italian films.

CALSTAR is a California-based air medical services company founded in 1983, providing critical air transport for patients across Northern and Central California and Northern Nevada. It operates a fleet of helicopters and airplanes, employing highly trained medical staff.

Saifuddin Azizi (12 March 1915 – 24 November 2003) was a Uyghur politician, revolutionary, and military leader who played a key role in the Soviet-backed Ili Rebellion and later served in prominent positions in the People's Republic of China. He was the first Chairman of the Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region (1955–1978) and held various leadership roles, including Vice Chairman of the National People's Congress (1954–1993) and Vice Chairman of the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference (1993–1998). A member of the Chinese Communist Party, he was instrumental in shaping Xinjiang's autonomy and governance.

Levon Zekiyan (born 1943) is an Armenian Catholic archbishop, scholar, and theologian. He graduated from the Mekhitarist Seminary of San Lazzaro degli Armeni and the Pontifical Gregorian University, earning degrees in philosophy and theology, and was ordained a priest in 1967. He completed his PhD at the State University of Istanbul in 1973, focusing on St. Augustine's theory of knowledge. Zekiyan has held prominent academic and administrative roles, including Principal of Moorat-Raphael College and Secretary of the Armenian Academy of the Mekhitarist Fathers. In 2014, Pope Francis appointed him apostolic administrator of the Armenian Catholic Archieparchy of Istanbul, elevating him to the rank of archbishop.

Hope High School, located in Hope, Arkansas, is a public secondary school established in 1931, succeeding Garland High School. It serves grades 9–12 within the Hope School District and has an enrollment of approximately 570 students. The school follows the Smart Core curriculum and offers Advanced Placement courses. The mascot is the Bobcat, and the school colors are red and white.

Greenhill is a neighborhood in Edinburgh, Scotland, that derives its name from the 16th-century Greenhill estate. The estate was originally owned by Thomas Aikenhead, an Edinburgh merchant. A stone carving of the former manor house can still be seen at the corner of Bruntsfield Place and Bruntsfield Gardens. The mausoleum of John Livingstone, a former laird of the estate, is also located in Greenhill. Since 2006, Greenhill has been part of a controlled parking zone.

The COVID-19 pandemic in Tunisia was part of the worldwide pandemic caused by SARS-CoV-2. The first case was confirmed on 2 March 2020 in Gafsa. By early 2021, Tunisia had recorded over 1.1 million cases and 29,000 deaths, with significant economic impacts.

Sadik Ahmad Turkistani is a Turkmenistani national who was held as an extrajudicial detainee at Guantanamo Bay from 2001 to 2006. Previously imprisoned and tortured by the Taliban in Kandahar, he was later sold to U.S. forces by the Northern Alliance. Though deemed not an "enemy combatant" by a U.S. tribunal, he remained detained until his 2006 transfer to Saudi Arabia, where he had lived before his capture. Turkistani was among several detainees who cultivated a secret garden at Guantanamo. After release, he resettled in Taif, Saudi Arabia.

Brahmachari is a 1992 Indian Tamil-language film directed by Muktha Srinivasan, featuring Nizhalgal Ravi and Gautami. The story follows Ganesan, a bachelor who falls in love with Malathi, navigating challenges posed by her wealthy family and a deception involving his uncle.

Grange Barn, located in Coggeshall, United Kingdom, is a Grade I listed building and one of the oldest surviving timber-framed buildings in Europe, dating back to the 13th century. Originally associated with Coggeshall Abbey, the barn remained in agricultural use until 1960, after which it fell into disrepair. In 1982, Braintree District Council acquired the barn and initiated its restoration, which was completed in 1985 and received a Europa Nostra award. In 1989, the barn was entrusted to the National Trust for preservation. Today, Grange Barn is open to the public, featuring exhibits on its history and local woodworking tools. It is also available for special events.

Manny Pacquiao vs. Jeff Horn was a boxing match for the WBO welterweight title held on July 2, 2017, at Suncorp Stadium in Brisbane, Australia. Horn won by controversial unanimous decision, sparking criticism from analysts and fans.
Gerhard vom Rath (1830–1888) was a German geologist and mineralogist known for his contributions to mineralogy and crystallography.

Newry railway station (Irish: *Stáisiún Iúr Cinn Trá*) is a railway station in Newry, Northern Ireland, operated by NI Railways. Opened in 1855, it was later renamed and closed before reopening in 1984. It serves the *Enterprise* express to Dublin and Belfast, along with local NIR services. A modern station building was completed in 2009.

Panhard was a French automobile manufacturer, founded in 1887 as Panhard et Levassor by René Panhard and Émile Levassor. It pioneered early automotive innovations, including the Panhard rod and sleeve valve engines. Known for luxury cars and military vehicles, it ceased civilian production in 1967 and was later acquired by Citroën and then Renault Trucks Defense. The company specialized in armored vehicles before its dissolution in 2018. The Panhard rod remains its most enduring legacy in automotive engineering.

The Aston Centre for Europe (ACE), established in 2009 at Aston University, is a research center co-directed by Professors Simon Green, John Gaffney, and Dr. Nathaniel Copsey. It focuses on European policy issues and collaborates with various European institutions for educational programs and training.

*Rajmohan's Wife* is a novella by Indian writer Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay, published in 1864. Written in English, it tells the story of a love triangle and a family feud in rural Bengal. The plot involves Rajmohan, his wife Matangini, and Madhav, who is in love with Matangini. The story explores themes of love, betrayal, and social conflict within a zamindar family.

Sobelair was a Belgian charter airline founded on 30 July 1946, with operations based at Brussels Airport. Initially focused on flights to the Belgian Congo, it later specialized in holiday charters. A subsidiary of Sabena from 1949 to 2001, Sobelair modernized its fleet over the decades, operating aircraft such as the Boeing 737 and 767. Following Sabena's bankruptcy in 2001, Sobelair faced financial difficulties, briefly operating under SN Brussels Airlines before declaring bankruptcy on 19 January 2004, ceasing operations after nearly 60 years.

Mordaunt Fenwick-Bisset (c. 1825 – 1884) was a British Member of Parliament for West Somerset. He was also Master of the Devon and Somerset Staghounds from 1855 to 1881, and was instrumental in increasing wild red deer numbers on Exmoor.

Acadia Pharmaceuticals, founded in 1993 and headquartered in San Diego, California, is a biopharmaceutical company specializing in developing treatments for central nervous system disorders. Its flagship product, Nuplazid (pimavanserin), was approved by the FDA in 2016 for treating hallucinations and delusions associated with Parkinson's disease psychosis. The company is also conducting clinical trials for other conditions, including dementia-related psychosis and major depressive disorder. Originally established as Receptor Technologies in Vermont, Acadia relocated to California in 1997 to foster industry partnerships. It went public in 2004 and has since expanded its portfolio, including acquiring CerSci Therapeutics in 2020.

Daniel James (April 17, 1801 – November 27, 1876) was an American-born British merchant and businessman. A key figure in Phelps, Dodge & Co., he managed its Liverpool branch, Phelps, James & Co., overseeing the export of metals and cotton between Britain and the U.S. He played a central role in relief efforts during the Cotton Famine of the 1860s. Naturalized as a British citizen in 1866, James faced financial crises and a major customs scandal in the 1870s. He left a lasting mercantile legacy before his death in Liverpool, where he was buried. His descendants expanded the family business into mining and railroads.

El Camino High School is a public high school located in Oceanside, California, established in 1975 and part of the Oceanside Unified School District. The school serves approximately 2,958 students with a faculty of 144, maintaining a student-to-teacher ratio of 26:1. The school's colors are brown and gold, and its mascot is the Wildcat. El Camino High School is known for its athletic programs within the CIF San Diego Section and the Avocado West League, and it has a rivalry with Oceanside High School. The school has garnered media attention for various incidents, including a 1986 controversy over books in its library and a dramatic anti-drunk driving program staged in 2008.
Recep Burak Yılmaz (born 27 November 1995) is a Turkish professional footballer who plays as a centre-back for Hatayspor.

Melukote, also known as Yadavagiri, is a municipal town and significant religious center located in the Mandya district of Karnataka, India. Situated approximately 36 km northwest of Mandya and 51 km north of Mysore, Melukote is renowned for its temples, particularly the Cheluva-Narayana Swamy Temple and the Yoga-Narasimha Temple, which attract numerous devotees. The town has historical significance as a prominent center of Srivaishnavism, especially during the 12th century when the saint Ramanujacharya resided there. Melukote is also known for its annual Vairamudi Brahmotsava festival, which draws hundreds of thousands of visitors. Additionally, the town is home to the Academy of Sanskrit Research and the Sri Veda Vedanta Bodhini Sanskrit College, contributing to its reputation as a center of learning and culture. The surrounding area includes the Melukote Temple Wildlife Sanctuary, which protects diverse wildlife and offers scenic landscapes.

The architecture of the Deccan sultanates reflects a rich cultural heritage and a blend of Islamic and indigenous styles, primarily seen in the regions of Ahmadnagar, Bidar, Bijapur, Golkonda, and Gulbarga. Notable structures in Ahmadnagar include the Farah Bakhsh Bagh and tombs of significant figures such as Salabat Khan. Bidar is renowned for its impressive Bidar Fort, the Mahmud Gawan Madrasa, and the Bahmani Tombs, showcasing intricate designs and grand scale. Bijapur features remarkable monuments like the Gol Gumbaz, which houses the second-largest dome in the world, and the elegant Ibrahim Rouza. Golkonda is famous for its formidable fortifications and the Jami Masjid, along with the royal necropolis of the Qutb Shahis. Lastly, Gulbarga, the initial capital of the Bahmani sultanate, is home to the Shah Bazar Masjid and the Haft Gumbad complex. Collectively, these architectural achievements highlight the artistic and engineering prowess of the Deccan sultanates during their reign.

The Cunningham Cabin is a historic structure located near Moose, Wyoming, within Grand Teton National Park. Built in 1885, it is an example of a sod-roofed, double-pen or dog-trot style cabin, a design originating in Appalachia. Constructed without nails or metal fastenings, the cabin features saddle-V-notched logs and a central breezeway flanked by two rooms. The site spans 10 acres and includes remnants of other structures, such as a barn, bunkhouse, and a house site, as well as pits that may have served as wells or privies. The cabin was reconstructed in 1956 to preserve its historical integrity.

The cabin is also notable for its association with a violent incident in 1892, known as the "Gunfight at the Bar Flying U Ranch." Two men accused of horse theft were killed by a posse under unclear circumstances, with the men's guilt and the identity of the alleged U.S. Marshal leading the posse remaining unverified. The Cunningham Cabin was added to the National Register of Historic Places on October 2, 1973, as part of the Grand Teton National Park Multiple Property Submission. It remains a significant example of early frontier architecture and a site of historical intrigue.

The Schöneberger Sängerknaben is a boys' choir founded by Gerhard Hellwig on November 12, 1947. Initially performing in various venues, the choir gained prominence when engaged by the Deutsche Oper Berlin for Richard Wagner's Tannhäuser. Over the years, they participated in over 3,100 opera performances, recorded music, and undertook more than 300 concert tours. The choir ceased operations following Hellwig's death on January 15, 2011.
South Bedfordshire was a non-metropolitan district in Bedfordshire, England, from 1974 to 2009. Formed by merging three districts, it was abolished and merged into Central Bedfordshire.
*Cinéaste* is a quarterly American film magazine founded in 1967 and based in New York City.

The Toro Rosso STR10 was a Formula One car designed by Toro Rosso for the 2015 season. Driven by Max Verstappen and Carlos Sainz Jr., it scored 67 points, achieving two fourth-place finishes. The team finished seventh in the Constructors' Championship.

Matthew Benedict Walker MBE (born 25 April 1978) is a British Paralympic swimmer who has won 11 Paralympic medals, including three golds in relay events, across four Games from 2000 to 2012. Competing in the S7 classification, he also earned numerous medals at World, European, and Commonwealth Championships. Walker was appointed Member of the Order of the British Empire (MBE) in 2009 for his contributions to disabled sport.

Adolph Ernst Knoch (born St. Louis, Missouri) was a German-American theologian and Bible translator known for developing the Concordant Translation of the New Testament. Raised in a bilingual household, he became involved with the Plymouth Brethren and later published the magazine "Unsearchable Riches." Knoch's theological views challenged traditional doctrines, particularly regarding Hell and the Trinity, and emphasized a dispensationalist perspective. He passed away in Los Angeles, California, in 1965.

The kidnapping of Alexandra Măceșanu and Luiza Melencu refers to the abduction of two teenage girls in Romania. Fifteen-year-old Alexandra Măceșanu went missing on July 24, and 18-year-old Luiza Melencu disappeared in April. Gheorghe Dincă was arrested and accused of kidnapping, raping, and murdering both girls. The case sparked public outrage and allegations of corruption and human trafficking.

Tales of Zestiria the X is a 2016–2017 anime television series produced by Ufotable, based on the *Tales of Zestiria* video game. Set in a world where humans and Seraphim once coexisted, the story follows a young man who can see Seraphim and dreams of restoring harmony between the two races. Directed by Haruo Sotozaki and featuring music by Motoi Sakuraba and Go Shiina, the series aired in two parts, with the first half premiering in July 2016 and the second in January 2017. The anime adapts elements from both *Tales of Zestiria* and *Tales of Berseria*, with most of the original game's voice cast reprising their roles. It was licensed internationally by Crunchyroll and Madman Entertainment.

Irving Trust was a commercial bank founded in 1851 in New York City, originally named the Irving Bank of the City of New York. It underwent several name changes and mergers, becoming the Irving Trust Company in 1929. The bank primarily served mid- and large-sized corporations and had a global presence. In 1988, Irving Trust was acquired by the Bank of New York, resulting in the formation of the United States' 12th largest bank at that time.

Mesa/Boogie is an American manufacturer of guitar amplifiers, founded in 1969 by Randall Smith in Petaluma, California. Initially known for hot-rodding Fender amplifiers, the company gained prominence with the development of the Mark I, the first high-gain guitar amplifier, released in 1972. Over the years, Mesa/Boogie expanded its product line to include various amplifier models, such as the Rectifier series, which became essential in modern rock music. In 2021, Mesa/Boogie was acquired by Gibson, with Smith continuing as a Master Designer. The company is recognized for its innovative designs and contributions to the music industry, particularly in the realm of electric guitar amplification.

John Patrick Rourke (born March 26, 1942) is a South African botanist known for his work on the Cape Flora, particularly the Proteaceae family. He worked at Kirstenbosch National Botanical Garden and was curator of the Compton Herbarium. Several plant species have been named in his honor.

South Burnie is a suburb of Burnie, Tasmania, located 2.5 km (2 mi) southeast of the city center. With a population of 331 (2016 census), it lies within the City of Burnie local government area. The region was historically part of Emu Bay Municipality, renamed in 1988. The C112 route (Massy-Greene Drive) terminates in South Burnie, connecting to the Bass Highway and providing access to the Ridgley Highway (B18). The area was named after William Burnie, a director of the Van Diemen's Land Company.

Margaret JoBeth Williams (born December 6, 1948) is an American actress and television director. Born in Houston, Texas, she began her career with a role on the children's television series *Jabberwocky*. She gained recognition for her roles in motion pictures such as *Stir Crazy* (1980), *Poltergeist* (1982), and *The Big Chill* (1983).

Williams has also been lauded for her performances in television movies, including *The Day After* (1983) and *My Name is Bill W.* (1989), earning Emmy nominations for her roles in *Adam* (1983) and *Baby M* (1988). In 1995, she received an Academy Award nomination for directing the short film *On Hope*. She has also had recurring roles in television series such as *Private Practice*.

Williams is married to director John Pasquin, and they have two children.

The *Codex Boturini*, also known as the *Tira de la Peregrinación de los Mexica*, is an early 16th-century Aztec manuscript that chronicles the migration of the Mexica people from Aztlán to the Basin of Mexico, culminating just before the founding of Tenochtitlan. Created on amate paper and folded accordion-style, the codex is notable for its unfinished state, with red and black ink used to depict date glyphs, footprints, and key events. It is attributed to a single tlacuilo (scribe) familiar with Aztec writing systems. Acquired by Lorenzo Boturini Benaduci in the 18th century, the codex is now housed in Mexico's National Museum of Anthropology. It shares similarities with other Aztec codices, such as the Aubin and Mexicanus Codices.

Ultrasound-modulated optical tomography (UOT) is an imaging technique used to visualize soft tissue by combining laser light and focused ultrasound. It offers greater penetration depth compared to traditional optical methods and relies on scattered photons within tissue to generate images. UOT operates through mechanisms involving ultrasonic modulation of light intensity, phase, and speckle patterns, enabling the reconstruction of internal tissue properties. Analytical models and advanced techniques, such as time-resolved frequency-swept UOT, enhance resolution and imaging capabilities. First proposed in 2013, UOT has potential applications in biomedical imaging, including virus detection.
Southfield F.C. was an amateur football club based in Birmingham, England, active from approximately 1881 to 1893.

Josephine is a feminine given name of Hebrew origin, meaning "(Jehovah) shall grow." Originally a French diminutive of *Joséphe*, it gained popularity in the 19th century, partly due to Empress Joséphine de Beauharnais. Common variants include *Josefina* (Spanish) and *Josefine* (Swedish), with nicknames like *Jo*, *Josie*, and *Fifi*.

A fish flake is a wooden rack used for drying salted cod in the open air, traditionally found in fishing villages, particularly in Newfoundland. Constructed with poles and sometimes covered with spruce boughs, flakes allow fish to cure under sunlight. Permanent flakes were built near fishing stages, while temporary "hand flakes" were used during abundant catches. This method was essential before refrigeration, ensuring fish preservation for market. Flakes remained in use in some communities into the late 20th century.

Randy Kaplan is an American singer-songwriter and musician known for his work in children's music and his eclectic musical style that blends American roots, country blues, and comedic storytelling. Born and raised in Dix Hills, Long Island, Kaplan studied philosophy and literature at the University of Michigan and UCLA before pursuing a career in acting and music. He has performed in various theater productions and television shows, and fronted the band “i” in the early 1990s. After relocating to New York City in 1993, he released his first CD, *Boyish Hips*, in 1997 and has since toured extensively across the United States. Kaplan's children's music has received critical acclaim, earning awards from NAPPA and recognition in various media outlets. He has released several albums, including *Mr. Diddie Wah Diddie* and *Jam on Rye*, and is noted for his engaging live performances. Kaplan currently resides in a Detroit suburb with his family, continuing to create music that appeals to both children and adults.

Nerima Wako-Ojiwa (born c. 1989) is a Kenyan political analyst and corporate executive. She is the Executive Director of Siasa Place, a youth-led organization she co-founded in 2015. She holds degrees from Jacksonville State University. Her husband and uncle are also involved in Kenyan politics.

The Roland Space Echo is a series of tape echo and delay effects units produced by Roland Corporation from 1973. Renowned for their warm, analog sound and innovative design, the Space Echo models, including the iconic RE-201, utilized a continuous tape loop system to create echo and reverb effects. Introduced by Roland founder Ikutaro Kakehashi, the series evolved from earlier tape echo designs, offering features like adjustable delay, reverb, and sound-on-sound recording. Later models, such as the RE-301 and RE-501, incorporated additional effects like chorus and improved noise reduction. The Space Echo remains highly regarded for its distinctive sound and influence on music production.

Siegfried Müller is a German former sidecarcross racer. With driver Eugen Siegle, he finished sixth in the 1974 FIM Cup and fifth in the 1976 FIM European Championship. Partnering with Reinhard Böhler, Müller won the inaugural 1980 FIM World Championship.

Qi Xin (born November 3, 1926) is a Chinese revolutionary and educator, best known as the widow of former Chinese Communist Party (CCP) leader Xi Zhongxun and the mother of current Chinese leader Xi Jinping. A veteran of the Second Sino-Japanese War, she studied at the Counter-Japanese Military and Political University and later worked in education and propaganda. She has been portrayed as a key influence on her family's political legacy.

Newfoundland French (français terre-neuvien) is a moribund dialect of French historically spoken in the Port au Port Peninsula of Newfoundland, Canada. It emerged from French settlements established during the 17th and 18th centuries, particularly influenced by Breton, Norman, and Basque fishermen. Despite the region's rich French heritage, the dialect has faced significant decline since the mid-20th century due to increased contact with English-speaking populations, the establishment of an American air force base in 1940, and a lack of French-language education. As a result, Newfoundland French is now spoken by fewer than 500 individuals, primarily elderly residents, while younger generations tend to speak Acadian French. Efforts are underway to revive the Newfoundland dialect, but current educational practices favor standard Quebec French or Acadian-influenced varieties. The community's historical and cultural significance remains, with approximately 15,000 descendants of French settlers still residing in the province.

The Council for the Central Laboratory of the Research Councils (CCLRC) was a UK non-departmental public body established in 1995 to manage large-scale scientific facilities and support research in materials, biomolecular science, space, and alternative energy. It merged with PPARC in 2007 to form the Science and Technology Facilities Council.

Lloyd Quinan (born 1957) is a Scottish politician, actor, and television producer. A member of the Scottish National Party (SNP), he served as a Member of the Scottish Parliament (MSP) for the West of Scotland from 1999 to 2003. Before entering politics, he worked in theatre and television, directing plays and presenting programs for STV and other broadcasters. An anti-nuclear campaigner, he was arrested during protests at Faslane. He later supported Scottish independence in the 2014 referendum.
The COVID-19 pandemic in Tunisia is part of the worldwide pandemic of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) caused by SARS-CoV-2. The first case in Tunisia was confirmed on March 2, 2020.

Banjara Hills is an upscale neighborhood in Hyderabad, Telangana, India, known for its luxurious lifestyle, commercial establishments, and historical significance. Originally developed in 1927 by Nawab Mehdi Nawaz Jung, a minister in the court of the last Nizam, the area was named after the Banjaras, its original inhabitants. The Nawab's residence, Banjara Bhavan, inspired by Antoni Gaudí's works, hosted notable figures like Jawaharlal Nehru and Rabindranath Tagore.

The area is renowned for its star hotels, including Taj Krishna, Taj Deccan, and Taj Banjara, as well as its diverse dining options and shopping malls like GVK One and City Center. Key landmarks include Jalagam Vengal Rao Park, KBR Park, and the 400-year-old Svayambhu Sri Lakshmi Narasimha Swamy Temple. Educational institutions like Muffakham Jah College of Engineering and Technology are also located here.

Banjara Hills is well-connected by TSRTC buses and nearby MMTS train stations, with improved infrastructure easing traffic congestion. Adjacent localities include Jubilee Hills, Somajiguda, and Panjagutta, making it a prominent hub in Hyderabad.

*Nyayangal Jayikkattum* is a 1990 Indian Tamil-language film directed and written by Sivachandran. Starring Sivakumar, Lakshmi, and Aishwarya in her Tamil debut, the film explores political enmity. The soundtrack was composed by Shankar–Ganesh with lyrics by Vaali. Released on 1 June 1990, it received mixed reviews, with praise for performances but criticism of certain scenes and logical flaws.

Michael Korovkin is an anthropologist specializing in political and economic anthropology, symbolic expression, and medical anthropology of stress. He has taught at universities in Canada and Italy and consulted for organizations including the UN. He is also the author of several novels, some published under pseudonyms, and books of poetry.

*Colorado Republican Federal Campaign Committee v. FEC*, 518 U.S. 604 (1996), was a case in which the Supreme Court of the United States held that independent expenditures by political parties, made without coordination with a candidate, are protected by the First Amendment. The case arose after the Colorado Republican Federal Campaign Committee purchased radio ads attacking a Democratic candidate. The Federal Election Commission (FEC) argued this violated the Federal Election Campaign Act of 1971 (FECA).

The Court's plurality opinion, delivered by Justice Breyer, found the ads were independent expenditures and thus protected. Justices Kennedy and Thomas concurred, arguing for broader First Amendment protections. Justice Stevens dissented, arguing for the government's interest in preventing corruption and leveling the electoral playing field. The case was later revisited in 2001, with the Court then ruling that coordinated expenditures by parties could be restricted.

Hamish McLachlan (born 28 August 1975) is an Australian sports broadcaster and television presenter for the Seven Network. Beginning his career in sports management, he transitioned to media in 2008, hosting *AFL Game Day* and co-commentating Australian Football League matches. McLachlan has hosted major sporting events, including the Olympics, Commonwealth Games, and Australian Open, and has written for the *Herald Sun*. Originally from Adelaide, he holds a commerce degree from the University of Adelaide and is the younger brother of AFL CEO Gillon McLachlan.

The Dornier Do 11 was a German heavy bomber developed in secret for the early Luftwaffe. Disguised as a freight transport, it served as a trainer for the still-secret air force. The Do 11 was the first German bomber to utilize two large engines, a departure from earlier designs. Plagued by problems, including wing vibrations and landing gear malfunctions, it was unpopular with pilots. The Do 11D was the last model to carry the Do 11 name. Its successors, the Do 13 and Do 23, also suffered from shortcomings and were eventually replaced by more advanced aircraft.

Hugh Alan Leslie OBE (17 April 1900 – 2 September 1974) was an Australian politician. He was a member of the Australian Parliament for Moore from 1949 to 1958, and again from 1961 to 1963. Prior to federal politics, Leslie served in the Western Australian Legislative Assembly.

Charles Jackson "Bud" Wildman (born June 3, 1946) is a Canadian politician. He was an Ontario New Democratic Party (NDP) member of the Legislative Assembly of Ontario for Algoma from 1975 to 1999. He served as a cabinet minister in the government of Bob Rae and as interim party leader in 1996.
Ivaylo Brusovski (born 1968) is a Bulgarian film director, producer, screenwriter, and actor.

Thomas Alexander Fraser, 12th Lord Lovat KT (17 June 1802 – 28 June 1875) was a Scottish peer and landowner. He served as Lord Lieutenant of Inverness from 1853 to 1873 and was made a Knight of the Thistle in 1865. Inheriting the Lovat estates in 1815, he was later confirmed as 12th Lord Lovat after the reversal of his ancestor's attainder.

Yaser Abdel Said (born January 27, 1957) is an Egyptian-American man convicted of the capital murders of his two daughters, Amina and Sarah Said, on January 1, 2008, in Irving, Texas. For nearly 12 years, he evaded capture, becoming a fugitive and was added to the FBI Ten Most Wanted Fugitives list on December 4, 2014. He was captured on August 26, 2020, in Justin, Texas.

Said's daughters had previously reported his abusive behavior, including physical and sexual abuse. On the day of the murders, Said lured his daughters into his taxi under the pretense of taking them to dinner, then drove them to Irving and shot them both. Sarah managed to call 911 before she died, identifying her father as the shooter.

After the murders, Said went into hiding, aided by his son, Islam Said, and his brother, Yassein Abdulfatah Said. Both were later convicted of conspiracy and harboring a fugitive. Yaser Said was found guilty of capital murder in August 2022 and sentenced to life in prison without parole. Islam was sentenced to 10 years in federal prison, and Yassein received a 12-year sentence. The case has been the subject of a documentary, "The Price of Honor," and was profiled on "The Hunt With John Walsh."

Empire Day was a 7,241 GRT cargo ship that was built in 1941 as a CAM ship by William Doxford & Sons Ltd, Sunderland, Co Durham, United Kingdom for the Ministry of War Transport (MoWT). In 1943, she was converted to a standard cargo ship. She served until August 1944 when she was torpedoed and sunk by U-198.

The Cunningham Cabin is a double-pen log cabin in Grand Teton National Park in the US state of Wyoming. It was built as a homestead in Jackson Hole and represents an adaptation of an Appalachian building form to the West. The cabin was built just south of Spread Creek by John Pierce Cunningham, who arrived in Jackson Hole in 1885 and subsisted as a trapper until he established the Bar Flying U Ranch in 1888. The Cunninghams left the valley for Idaho in 1928, when land was being acquired for the future Grand Teton National Park.

Cunningham and his wife grew about 100 acres (40 ha) of hay, later irrigating another 140 acres (57 ha) to provide feed for 100 cattle and eight horses. His brother, W. Pierce Cunningham, settled his family nearby. By 1924 the Cunningham ranch comprised 560 acres (230 ha). By 1926 Cunningham had moved out of cattle and was raising sheep on the land.

J. Pierce Cunningham was one of the original county commissioners chosen when Teton County was organized in 1923. He was also, at various times, justice of the peace, postmaster and game warden.

After 1895 the Cunninghams, who had built a more commodious house, used the cabin as a barn or a smithy. A small fortification was erected in 1895 during unrest involving the Bannack Indians. Traces of foundations survive.

16th Street Park, also known as Dominico – 16th Street Park, is a municipal park in Bayonne, New Jersey. It is located on the west side of the city along the Newark Bay across from Port Newark–Elizabeth Marine Terminal. It is a component of the Hackensack RiverWalk.

Gregory Brian Standridge (May 6, 1967 – November 16, 2017) was an American politician who served as a Republican member of the Arkansas State Senate for District 16, which includes Newton and Pope counties and parts of Boone, Carroll and Van Buren counties in the northern portion of his state.

Antony Thekkek, also known as Thampy Antony, is an Indian-American film actor, writer, activist and producer. As a writer he has published books in Malayalam and English. He is the author of the books Koonampara County, Life of Ouso, Lady Biker and Vasco da Gama. As an actor, he is active primarily in Malayalam cinema. He is also the elder brother of Malayalam actor Babu Antony. He is the first Malayalam actor to own a Tesla car. The actor has won the Basheer Amma Malayalam Award for his book Vasco da Gama.

Ultimate Canada is a not-for-profit organization that serves as the governing body of the sport of Ultimate (also known as "Ultimate Frisbee") in Canada. It runs the Canadian Ultimate Championships (CUC) and Canadian University Ultimate Championship (CUUC) series.

Canadian Ultimate Championships (CUC)

Each August, teams from across the country travel to the Canadian Ultimate Championships (CUC) to compete for the national title in 7 different divisions: mixed, open, women's, junior open, junior women, masters open and masters women. Teams compete at this seven-day tournament not only to determine the national champion, but also to determine who will represent Canada at the next world championships.

From 2016 to 2019, the mixed divisions were held as a separate event from the remaining divisions.

In 2022, the format was changed again with the three Masters divisions at their own event, the three senior divisions at their own event, and the two Junior divisions at their own event.

Canadian University Ultimate Championships (CUUC)

The CUUC started in 1995 and brings university teams from across the country to compete in the open & the women's division. Each fall Ultimate Canada operates two competitions for university Ultimate teams in Canada: the Canadian University Ultimate Championships (CUUC) and the Canadian Eastern University Ultimate Championships (CEUUC). The CEUUC began in 1998 and brings university teams primarily from Ontario and Quebec together to compete in the open & the women's division.

Adriaan van Dis (Bergen aan Zee, 16 December 1946) is a Dutch author. He debuted in 1983 with the novella Nathan Sid. In 1995 his book Indische Duinen (My Father's War), which in its narrative is a follow up to his debut novella, was also awarded several prestigious literary awards.

He is also known as the host of his own award-winning television talkshow named Hier is. Adriaan van Dis, that lasted from 1983 to 1992 and several successful award-winning television documentaries.

With the publication of his Indies inspired compilation book De Indie boeken (The Indies books) in 2012, van Dis establishes himself as one of the most significant second generation authors of Dutch Indies literature.

Hyundai Chungun High School (Korean: 현대청운고등학교; HCU), is a private boarding high school located in Dong-gu, Ulsan, South Korea. It is one of the six independent private high schools (자율형 사립고) in South Korea that require an application of nationwide scale and admissions test to the applicant students. Since students who want to get in HCU usually get straight A when they are at middle school and take a test to get in HCU, they usually get rank 1 or rank 2 for their grades of CSAT(대학수학능력시험)College Scholastic Ability Test statistically. For example, there was a research for those who took CSAT in 2016(2017학년도 대학수학능력시험), and HCU placed the 4th in nationwide for the average CSAT grade for Korean, Mathematics, and English. The percentage of rank 1 and rank 2 is 4%, 7% respectively. The aggregate percentile for rank 2 is 11%. Academic grading in South Korea 72.6% of students at HCU in 2016 got rank 1 or rank 2 for their CSAT grade.

La Grosse Décharge Est is a tributary of the north shore of La Grosse Décharge Ouest, flowing entirely in the town of Baie-Saint-Paul, in the Charlevoix Regional County Municipality, in the administrative region of Capitale-Nationale, in the province of Quebec, in Canada.

This valley is mainly served by secondary forest roads which connect to the Chemin du Séminaire which runs along the south side of La Grosse Décharge Ouest. Forestry is the main economic activity in this valley; recreational tourism, second.

The surface of La Grosse Décharge Est is generally frozen from the beginning of December until the beginning of April; however, safe circulation on the ice is generally done from mid-December to the end of March. The water level of the river varies with the seasons and the precipitation; the spring flood generally occurs in April.

Error concealment is a technique used in signal processing that aims to minimize the deterioration of signals caused by missing data, called packet loss. A signal is a message sent from a transmitter to a receiver in multiple small packets. Packet loss occurs when these packets are misdirected, delayed, resequenced, or corrupted.

The Asian Qualification Tournament for the 2020 Men's Olympic Volleyball Tournament was a volleyball tournament for men's national teams held in Jiangmen, China from 7 to 12 January 2020. 8 teams played in the tournament and the winners Iran qualified for the 2020 Summer Olympics (postponed to 2021 due to the COVID-19 pandemic).

Canal Park is a baseball stadium located in Akron, Ohio, United States, that is the home of the Akron RubberDucks of the Eastern League. The team is a double-A minor-league affiliate of the Cleveland Guardians. Opened in 1997, the stadium was designed by Populous, then known as HOK Sport, the same architectural firm that designed the Guardians' Progressive Field, which opened three years earlier. The stadium takes its name from its location adjacent to the Ohio and Erie Canal, which runs behind the left-field wall.

Titus Lucretius Carus (/ˈtaɪtəs luːˈkriːʃəs/ TY-təs loo-KREE-shəs, IPA-la; c. 99 – c. 55 BC) was a Roman poet and philosopher. His only known work is the philosophical poem De rerum natura, a didactic work about the tenets and philosophy of Epicureanism, and which usually is translated into English as On the Nature of Things and somewhat less often as On the nature of the universe. Lucretius has been credited with originating the concept of the three-age system that was formalised in 1836 by C. J. Thomsen.

Very little is known about Lucretius's life; the only certainty is that he was either a friend or client of Gaius Memmius, to whom the poem was addressed and dedicated.

De rerum natura was a considerable influence on the Augustan poets, particularly Virgil (in his Aeneid and Georgics, and to a lesser extent on the Eclogues) and Horace. The work was almost lost during the Middle Ages, but was rediscovered in 1417 in a monastery in Germany by Poggio Bracciolini and it played an important role both in the development of atomism (Lucretius was an important influence on Pierre Gassendi) and the efforts of various figures of the Enlightenment era to construct a new Christian humanism. Lucretius's scientific poem On the Nature of Things (c. 60 BC) has a remarkable description of Brownian motion of dust particles in verses 113–140 from Book II. He uses this as a proof of the existence of atoms.

The Faculty of Informatics and Statistics (FIS VŠE), also known as the School of Informatics and Statistics, is the fourth of six faculties at Prague University of Economics and Business. The faculty was established in 1991, following the dissolution of the Faculty of Direction. Its academic focus is informatics, statistics, econometrics and other mathematical methods applied to business practice. The faculty has eight departments and several research laboratories, and hosts around 2,500 students across its programs.

Molding sand, also known as foundry sand, is a sand that when moistened and compressed or oiled or heated tends to pack well and hold its shape. It is used in the process of sand casting for preparing the mould cavity.

Ebenezer Norman is a Liberian philanthropist, humanitarian, public speaker, and founder of the education non-profit A New Dimension of Hope (NDHope). He is involved in humanitarian efforts in Liberia and throughout West Africa, notably for efforts to build schools in attenuated or war-torn communities after the Second Liberian Civil War in 2003 and the Ebola virus epidemic in Liberia in 2015.

An acoustic torpedo is a torpedo that aims itself by listening for characteristic sounds of its target or by searching for it using sonar (acoustic homing). Acoustic torpedoes are usually designed for medium-range use, and often fired from a submarine.

The first passive acoustic torpedoes were developed nearly simultaneously by the United States Navy and the Germans during World War II. The Germans developed the G7e/T4 Falke, which was first deployed by the submarines U-603, U-758 and U-221 in March 1943. Few of these torpedoes were actually used and quickly phased out of service in favor of the T4's successor, the G7es T5 Zaunkönig torpedo in August 1943. The T5 first saw widespread use in September 1943 against North Atlantic escort vessels and merchant ships in convoys.

On the Allied side, the US Navy developed the Mark 24 mine, which was actually an aircraft launched, anti-submarine passive acoustic homing torpedo. The first production Mk. 24s were delivered to the U.S. Navy in March 1943, and it scored its first verified combat kills in May 1943. About 204 torpedoes were launched against submarine targets, with 37 Axis submarines being sunk and a further 18 damaged.

Since its introduction, the acoustic torpedo has proven to be an effective weapon against surface ships as well as serving as an anti-submarine weapon. Today, acoustic torpedoes are mostly used against submarines.

Azerbaijan—France relations are the bilateral relations between the Republic of Azerbaijan and the French Republic in the political, socio-economic, cultural, and other spheres.

Cooperation is carried out in such areas as energy (Total), environment (Suez CNIM), transport (Alstom, Thales, Iveco, SYSTRA), agriculture (Lactalis, Danone), space industry (Airbus, Thales), gas sector, security, culture, hotel business (Accor), banking (Societe Generale), aviation, etc.

The 1952–53 season was Fussball Club Basel 1893's 59th season in their existence. It was their seventh consecutive season in the top flight of Swiss football after their promotion from the Nationalliga B the season 1945–46. They played their home games in the Landhof, in the Wettstein Quarter in Kleinbasel. Jules Düblin was the club's chairman for the seventh successive season.

A mercy rule, slaughter rule, knockout rule, or skunk rule ends a two-competitor sports competition earlier than the scheduled endpoint if one competitor has a very large and presumably insurmountable scoring lead over the other. It is called the mercy rule because it spares further humiliation for the loser. It is common in youth sports in North America, where running up the score is considered unsporting. It is especially common in baseball and softball in which there is no game clock and a dominant team could in theory continue an inning endlessly.

The rules vary widely, depending on the level of competition, but nearly all youth sports leagues and high school sports associations and many college sports associations in the United States have mercy rules for sports including baseball, softball, American football and association football.

However, mercy rules usually do not take effect until a prescribed point in the game (like the second half of an association football game). Thus, one team, particularly if it is decidedly better than a weaker opponent, can still "run up the score" before the rule takes effect. For instance, in American football, one team could be ahead by 70 points with three minutes left in the first half; in baseball, the better team could have a 20-run lead in the second inning, but the game would still continue.
"Like a Man" is a song recorded by Onhel, featuring American rapper Lil Wayne. It was released as a single on August 8, 2017.
Mouse-Warming is a 1952 Warner Bros. Looney Tunes short directed by Chuck Jones. The cartoon was released on September 8, 1952 and stars Claude Cat in his final solo cartoon.

The London Midland and Scottish Railway (LMS) Hughes Crab or Horwich Mogul is a class of mixed-traffic 2-6-0 steam locomotive built between 1926 and 1932. They are noted for their appearance with large steeply-angled cylinders to accommodate a restricted loading gauge.
Robert C. Morgan (born 1943) is an American art critic, art historian, curator, poet, and artist.

The Geologist is an glue paint on paper on canvas painting by the German painter Carl Spitzweg executed c. 1860. It belongs to the collection of the Von der Heydt Museum in Wuppertal, to which it was bequeathed in 1913. Spitzweg's style of painting belongs to the late Romantic period.

Margaret C. McEntee SR. SC., also known by her confirmation name Marita James, (born July 10, 1935) is an American Catholic religious sister and educator who is known as being the inspiration for the character of Sister James in the Pulitzer Prize and Tony Award-winning play Doubt: A Parable by John Patrick Shanley. The 2008 film adaptation is dedicated to her.

Thrilling is an online marketplace for vintage clothing, apparel and home goods. The company partners with independent vintage and secondhand clothing stores that use the platform to list, sell, and ship inventory. Thrilling was founded by Shilla Kim-Parker and Brad Mallow in 2018, In 2021, the company received $8.47 million in series A funding.

Thrilling has partnered with Banana Republic, Academy Award nominated costume designer Ruth E. Carter, and offers a studio service that uses the company's network of stores to find authentic period clothing for film and television productions. In 2020, Thrilling partnered with stylist Zerina Akers of "Black Owned Everything" to curate an online collection of vintage items from black-owned stores.

As of January 2022, Thrilling hosted listings from more than 900 vintage stores in approximately 200 cities and planned to use series A funding to improve technology such as partner store analytics, partially automating the item listing process, and adding personalization for customers shopping on the site.

In May 2022, Thrilling introduced a home goods section that allows thrift shop owners to list and sell vintage home decor items.
Dennis Mallonee (born January 22, 1955) is an American writer of comic books.

O RLY? is an Internet phenomenon, typically presented as an image macro featuring a snowy owl. The phrase "O RLY?", an abbreviated form of "Oh, really?", is popularly used in Internet forums in a sarcastic manner, often in response to an obvious, predictable, or blatantly false statement. Similar owl image macros followed the original to present different views, including images with the phrases "YA RLY" (Yeah, really.), "NO WAI!!" (No way!), and NO RLY. (Not really.)

Bobby is a 2002 Indian Telugu-language romance film written and directed by Sobhan and produced by K. Krishna Mohan Rao. The movie stars Mahesh Babu, Aarthi Agarwal, Raghuvaran, and Prakash Raj while Brahmanandam, Sunil, Pragathi, Ravi Babu, and Meher Ramesh play supporting roles. The soundtrack of the movie was scored by Mani Sharma, the cinematography was done by Venkat R. Prasad, and editing was handled by S. Sudhakar Reddy. The film released on 31 October 2002.

Tomás Francisco Reilly (December 20, 1908 – July 21, 1992) was an American-born bishop in the Catholic Church. He served as Prelate of San Juan de la Maguana in the Dominican Republic from 1956–1969 and the first bishop of the Diocese of San Juan de la Maguana from 1969–1977.

The Abbey of San Pietro al Monte is an small medieval monastic complex mostly dating to the 11th century, in Lombard Romanesque style, on a hill outside the town of Civate, province of Lecco, in northern Italy.

Founded in 796 as a small hermitage by the Lombard king Desiderius, the complex was considerably expanded in the 11th century, and then left alone in terms of major building and decorating. It was secularized in the aftermath of the French Revolution. Its 11th-century features remain very largely intact, including many frescos and carved reliefs.

In 2016 it was added to the "tentative" list to be a UNESCO World Heritage Site, as part of a group of eight Italian medieval Benedictine monasteries, representing "The cultural landscape of the Benedictine settlements in medieval Italy".

The Regional Arts & Culture Council (RACC) is an organization that administers arts grants in Multnomah, Washington, and Clackamas Counties that also do advocacy in the Portland metropolitan area in Oregon, United States. It evolved from the city’s Metropolitan Arts Commission agency in the 1990s. In 1995, the Metropolitan Arts Commission became the RACC as an independent non-profit organization.

David Peter Ryan (born 5 January 1957) is an English former professional footballer who played as a goalkeeper. He played in the English Football League for Port Vale in 1976, on loan from Manchester United. He later played non-League football for Southport, Northwich Victoria, Hyde United and Chorley.

Benedicto “Bong” Ducat (born May 6, 1957) is a Filipino impressionist painter. He is now teaching at the University of Santo Tomas College of Fine Arts and Design. He is noted for his renderings of plants, flowers and nature in droplet-like brush strokes and bright colors. In college, Ducat won the University of Santo Tomas Benavides Civic Award.

Dennis Knight (born December 26, 1968) is an American chef and retired professional wrestler. He is best known for his appearances with World Championship Wrestling between 1992 and 1994 under the ring name Tex Slazenger and with the World Wrestling Federation (WWF, now WWE) between 1996 and 2001 under the ring names Phineas I. Godwinn and Mideon, as well as his real name.

A homewrecker (sometimes styled as home wrecker or home-wrecker) is a person, object or activity that causes or comes close to causing the breakup of a marriage (or similar partnership). The homewrecker is said to have taken one of the spouses away from the marriage, thus "wrecking" the marital home.

Most commonly, the label "homewrecker" is applied to a person having an affair with someone else's spouse or domestic partner; it can also refer to other forces that are destructive to a marital relationship and tied only to one party to that relationship.

Joseph Hamilton (August 28, 1938 – August 3, 2021) was an American professional wrestler, promoter, and trainer. He was best known as one half of the tag team The Assassins where he was called "Assassin #1". When the Assassins ended, He wrestled as "The Assassin" and "The Flame" both while wearing a mask.

Hamilton teamed up with Tom Renesto in late 1961 to form the masked heel (bad guy) team known as "The Assassins" and, for over a decade, he and Renesto wrestled all over the world with great success. Once Renesto retired, Hamilton kept the Assassins team alive by partnering with wrestlers such as Randy Colley and "Hercules" Hernandez under the trademark gold and black masks of the Assassins.

In the late 1980s, Hamilton founded Deep South Wrestling (DSW), where he also wrestled until a back injury forced him to retire in 1988. After Deep South Wrestling closed, he worked extensively for World Championship Wrestling both on the booking team and later on as a trainer at WCW's Power Plant training facility. In 2005, he reopened Deep South Wrestling, this time as an affiliate of World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE), to train wrestlers the WWE had signed to developmental contracts. In 2007, WWE abruptly ended their contract with DSW and the promotion suspended operations later in the year.
Bethuel Muzeu (born 2 February 2000), also known as Muzeu Muzeu, is a Namibian footballer who plays for National First Division club Black Leopards and the Namibia national team.

Shigeichi Nagano (長野 重一, Nagano Shigeichi, March 30, 1925 – January 30, 2019) was a Japanese photographer. He won the Ina Nobuo Award in 1986 and had a major retrospective at the Tokyo Metropolitan Museum of Photography in 2000.

The Children's Museum of the Arts (“CMA”) is located at 103 Charlton Street, Manhattan, New York, United States in the South Village district. Founded by Kathleen Schneider in 1988, CMA opened its new, 10,000-square-foot space in October 2011.

At CMA, kids ages 0–15 work with teaching artists to create art, including drawings, sculpture, sound art, textiles, and stop-motion animation. As part of its mission to promote art for all children, CMA features programs and partnerships designed for children with learning disabilities, children in foster care, and children in homeless families.

The museum maintains a collection of over 2,000 pieces of international children's art dating back to the 1930s from over 50 different countries.
Maria Silva Cruz (20 April 1915 – 23 August 1936) was a Spanish anarchist and a hero of the Casas Viejas Uprising in Spain. She was also known as "La Libertaria."

Loop (ループ, Rūpu) is a horror novel by Japanese writer Koji Suzuki, and the third in his series of Ring.

The story revolves around a simulated reality, exactly the same as our own, known as the Loop: created to simulate the emergence and evolution of life. It is in this alternate universe that the events of the previous novels, Ring and Spiral took place.

Loop is the only book in the series to have not been the basis of any Ring films.
The unicameral National Assembly (Portuguese: Assembleia Nacional) is the legislative body of the Republic of Cabo Verde.

Richard Gordon Guindon (December 2, 1935 – February 27, 2022) was an American cartoonist best known for his gag panel Guindon. Guindon's cartoons have appeared in the Minneapolis Tribune, The Realist, and the Detroit Free Press.

Joanne M. Garvey (1935–2014) was an attorney in San Francisco, California, and was a nationally recognized expert in field of taxation. She was the first woman to serve on the California State Bar Board of Governors from 1971–1974, and was one of the founders of the California Women Lawyers organization in 1974. Garvey also was the first female president of the San Francisco Barristers, and in 1981 became the first female president of the Bar Association of San Francisco since the Association’s founding in 1872. In addition, she served in a number of leadership positions with different sections and committees of the American Bar Association, and as a member of its Board of Governors and House of Delegates. Moreover, Garvey helped form the State Bar of California's Taxation Section, from which she also received the Section’s lifetime achievement award in 1994—the Joanne M. Garvey Award—which was named for her. In 2003, Garvey received the American Bar Association Commission on Women's 2003 Margaret Brent Women Lawyers of Achievement Award. After Garvey’s death in 2014, Charles Rettig—prior to becoming Commissioner of the Internal Revenue Service—stated that: "From every perspective, Joanne Garvey was a legend. She was among the most highly respected lawyers in the United States (tax or otherwise)".

Aurélien Bayard Chedjou Fongang (IPA-fr; born 20 June 1985) is a Cameroonian former professional footballer who played as a centre back for LOSC Lille, Galatasaray, Bursaspor, Adana Demirspor and the Cameroon national team.

James David Bouknight (/ˈbʊknaɪt/; born September 18, 2000) is an American professional basketball player for the Charlotte Hornets of the National Basketball Association (NBA). He played college basketball for the UConn Huskies.

The 2014 FedEx Cup Playoffs was the series of four golf tournaments that determined the season champion on the U.S.-based PGA Tour. It was played from August 21 to September 14 and comprised the following four events:

These were the eighth FedEx Cup playoffs since their inception in 2007.

The point distributions can be seen here.

On July 9, 2014, a mass shooting occurred in a home located in northern Harris County, Texas, near the Spring census-designated place, a suburban area of the Greater Houston area, leaving six family members dead, four of them children, and a lone survivor. 34-year old Ronald Lee Haskell, was apprehended after a standoff that lasted several hours. Haskell was related to the victims via his ex-wife.

The Kilwa Sultanate (Lang-fa) was a sultanate, centered at Kilwa (an island off modern-day, Kilwa District in Lindi Region of Tanzania), whose authority, at its height, stretched over the entire length of the Swahili Coast. According to the legend, it was founded in the 10th century by Ali ibn al-Hassan Shirazi, a Persian prince of Shiraz. His family ruled the Sultanate until the year 1277. They were replaced by the Arab family of Abu Moaheb until 1505, when they were overthrown by a Portuguese invasion. By 1513, the sultanate was already fragmented into smaller states, many of which became protectorates of the Sultanate of Oman.

Srikanth Kidambi (born 7 February 1993) is an Indian badminton player. A former world no. 1, Kidambi was awarded the Padma Shri, India's fourth highest civilian award, in 2018. and the Arjuna award in 2015. In 2021, he became the first Indian to reach the World Championship final in the men's singles discipline.

Colonel Sir John Wallace Pringle, CB, FRGS (23 May 1863 – 16 July 1938) was a British engineer who was Chief Inspecting Officer of the Railways Inspectorate of the Ministry of Transport from 1916 to 1929. As such he was in charge of investigations into a number of serious railway accidents in the UK.

Hainan HNA Infrastructure Investment Group Co., Ltd. formerly Hainan Island Construction Co., Ltd. is a Chinese listed company based in Haikou. in mid-2016 the company acquired HNA Infrastructure Group (Chinese: 海航基础产业集团) in a reverse IPO from intermediate parent company HNA Infrastructure Holding Group (Chinese: 海航基础控股集团), which HNA Infrastructure Group is the parent company of HNA Real Estate and HNA Airport Group (Chinese: 海航机场集团, in turn HNA Airport Group is the parent company of HNA Airport Holdings (Group) (Chinese: 海航机场控股(集团)) and HNA Airport Holdings is the parent company of Sanya Phoenix International Airport Co., Ltd.); HNA Airport Group is the largest shareholder of Haikou Meilan International Airport (19.58% as at October 2016), which in turn the largest shareholder of Hong Kong listed company HNA Infrastructure. In December 2016 a proposed capital increase of the HK-listed company was announced. HNA Infrastructure Investment Group would purchase a minority share directly. Haikou Meilan International Airport was the second-largest shareholder of Hainan Airlines; Hainan Airlines, however, also owned a minority stake in Haikou Meilan International Airport.

The registered office was located in Meilan District, but the general office was located in West Zone 21/F, HNA Building on 7 Guoxing Avenue.

Anna Magdalena Appel, (later known as Freifrau Anna Magdalena von Hochstädten), (Darmstadt, 8 March 1846-Wiesbaden, 19 December 1917) was a German ballet dancer, morganatic wife to Louis III, Grand Duke of Hesse.

The Space Force Association (SFA) is an independent, 501(c)(3) non-profit organization that serves as a professional military association, space advocacy group, and space education association for the United States Space Force and space professionals at large.
James Fitton R.A. (11 February 1899 – 2 May 1982) was an English painter, lithographer and theatre set designer, and a founder member of the left-wing Artists' International Association.

The 2014 Mountain Province Bus accident was a bus accident that occurred when a Florida Trans bus fell off a road in Sitio Paggang, Talubin, Bontoc, Mountain Province of the Philippines. The accident occurred on February 7, 2014. Fourteen people were killed and more than 32 people were injured. Comedian Tado and two more artists died in this accident.

Wrocław Global Forum (WGF) is an annual transatlantic conference centered on topics related to democracy, prosperity and security. Launched in 2010, the event is held in Wrocław, Poland. It is organized by the Atlantic Council, the Polish Institute of International Affairs and the city of Wrocław. The most recent Forum took place on June 2–3, 2016.

Theresa Park (born 1967) is a New York-based literary agent. She discovered and represents several internationally best-selling authors, including Nicholas Sparks, Debbie Macomber, Emily Giffin, Janice Y.K. Lee, Taylor Jenkins Reid, Cecelia Ahern, and Deborah Harkness, among others.

She grew up principally in Germany, Lebanon, and Austria, but attended UC Santa Cruz and Harvard Law School in the United States after graduating from the American International School in Vienna, Austria. She is fluent in German and can speak French.
Herbert Willoughby Ellis FRES FGS (28 June 1869-15 October 1943) was a British entomologist.

The Mississippi Wing of Civil Air Patrol (CAP) is the highest echelon of Civil Air Patrol in the state of Mississippi. Mississippi Wing headquarters are located in Jackson, Mississippi. The Mississippi Wing consists of over 500 cadet and adult members at over 16 locations across the state of Mississippi.

The Czech National Bank, (Lang-cs, ČNB) is the central bank and financial market supervisor in the Czech Republic, headquartered in Prague. It is and a member of the European System of Central Banks. It was established on 1 January 1993 from the division of the State Bank of Czechoslovakia as part of the process of dissolution of Czechoslovakia, together with the National Bank of Slovakia.

In accordance with its primary objective, the CNB sets monetary policy, issues banknotes and coins and manages the circulation of the Czech koruna, the payment system and settlement between banks. It also performs supervision of the banking sector, the capital market, the insurance industry, pension funds, credit unions and electronic money institutions, as well as foreign exchange supervision.

Adolph Ernst Knoch (December 19, 1874 – March 28, 1965) was the author of numerous theological writings and a Bible publisher.

Knoch founded the Concordant Publishing Concern and translated the Concordant Version of the Bible.
Two Paddocks is a wine producer based in Central Otago, New Zealand. It is owned and operated by actor Sam Neill.

Armenia Marriott Hotel Yerevan (Lang-hy), is a hotel at the central Kentron District of Yerevan, Armenia. It was opened in 1958 as Armenia Hotel, a state-owned enterprise during the Soviet period. After the collapse of the Soviet Union, the hotel was privatized in 1998 and following a major renovation; it was reopened as the Armenia Marriott Hotel Yerevan in 1999.

The hotel is located on 1 Amiryan Street, overlooking the Republic Square. The National Gallery and the government building are located in front of the hotel.

As of 2016, with 259 guestrooms, Armenia Marriott is the 2nd-largest hotel in Armenia. As of 2020, the hotel has 144 rooms in the inventory with the Ararat wing being temporarily closed.

Philip Brandon Martin (born October 31, 1978), better known as Bishop Lamont, is an American rapper from Carson, California. He was signed to Dr. Dre's Aftermath Entertainment record label and released one project under the label, eventually leaving in 2010.

SH-SY5Y is a human derived cell line used in scientific research. The original cell line, called SK-N-SH, from which it was subcloned was isolated from a bone marrow biopsy taken from a four-year-old female with neuroblastoma. SH-SY5Y cells are often used as in vitro models of neuronal function and differentiation. They are adrenergic in phenotype but also express dopaminergic markers and, as such, have been used to study Parkinson's disease, neurogenesis, and other characteristics of brain cells.

The 2017 Ole Miss Rebels football team represented the University of Mississippi in the 2017 NCAA Division I FBS football season. The Rebels played their home games at Vaught–Hemingway Stadium in Oxford, Mississippi and competed in the Western Division of the Southeastern Conference (SEC). They were led by then-interim head coach Matt Luke. They finished the season 6–6, 3–5 in SEC play to finish in sixth place in the Western Division.

On February 22, 2017, the University of Mississippi self-imposed a one-year ban from post-season play on the Ole Miss football team for the 2017 season, due to alleged NCAA violations. On July 20, 2017, head coach Hugh Freeze resigned in the wake of allegations about repeated calls to a female escort service from his school-issued cell phone. He was given the ultimatum, either resign or be fired.
William Horsell (31 March 1807 – 23 December 1863) was an English hydrotherapist, publisher, and temperance and vegetarianism activist. Horsell published the first vegan cookbook in 1849.
Chuck Wichgers (born July 4, 1965) is a Wisconsin politician and businessman. He is a Republican.

John Becker (born April 17, 1968) is an American college basketball coach, currently the head coach of the Vermont Catamounts of the America East Conference. He replaced Mike Lonergan, who left to become the coach at George Washington University.

"Bizarre Love Triangle" is a song by the English rock band New Order, released as a single in 1986 from their fourth studio album, Brotherhood (1986), which reached the top five on the US Hot Dance Music/Club Play Singles chart, and No. 5 on the Australian ARIA Charts (No. 1 on the Victoria state chart) in March 1987.

It failed to make the top 40 in either the United Kingdom (only reaching No. 56) or the US Billboard Hot 100. In the United States, the song reached No. 8 on the Hot Dance Music/Maxi-Singles Sales chart, but failed to chart on the Hot 100 during its original 1986 release. However, a new mix included on The Best of New Order was released in 1994 and finally made a brief appearance on the Billboard Hot 100 in the No. 98 position in 1995.

In 2004, the song was ranked No. 204 in Rolling Stone's "The 500 Greatest Songs of All Time."
Chillicothe is a city in Hardeman County, in the U.S. state of Texas. The population was 707 at the 2010 census.

Henry Stuart Russell (16 March 1818 – 5 March 1889) was an English-born explorer, politician, historian and pastoralist, best known for establishing the Cecil Plains Station around the Condamine River area of Australia.

Nancy Duff Campbell is an American lawyer and a founder and co-president of the National Women's Law Center. Campbell has focused on women's law and public policy issues and has participated in the development of legislative initiatives and litigation regarding women's rights, emphasizing issues affecting low‐income women, and has authored articles on women's legal issues.

Ranpak Holdings Corp. manufactures machines and paper products used in protective paper-based packing for shipping goods and merchandise for e-commerce and industry, along with automation solutions. The company is based in Concord, Ohio, and has production facilities and offices in Reno, Nevada; Raleigh, North Carolina; Kansas City, Missouri; the Netherlands; Czech Republic; Shanghai and Singapore. The paper packaging material can be used for multiple applications like wrapping, cushioning, void fill and cold chain purposes, and is sometimes known as dunnage. Ranpak counts customers globally like Amazon, IKEA and Walmart.
Abderahmane Hachoud (Lang-ar; born July 2, 1988, in El Attaf) is an Algerian professional football player.

Fairhope Plantation is a historic Carpenter Gothic plantation house and historic district, located one mile east of Uniontown, Alabama, US. The 2+1⁄2-story wood-framed main house was built in the Gothic Revival style in the late 1850s. The plantation historic district includes six other contributing buildings, in addition to the main house. It was added to the Alabama Register of Landmarks and Heritage on December 19, 1991, and subsequently to the National Register of Historic Places on May 29, 1992, due to its architectural and historical significance.

Princess Jane Mpologoma Nabanakulya is a daughter of Uganda's first President Mutesa II of Buganda. She was born in Sunga Village, Buyaga County, Bunyoro-Kitara Kingdom, on April 12, 1964 by Omuzaana Naome Nanyonga, a midwife of Major General Sir Mutesa II of Buganda.
Lev Abramovich Dodin (Lang-ru, born 1944) is a modern Russian theater director, the leader of Saint Petersburg Maly Drama Theater.

Otto Eisenschiml (June 16, 1880 – December 7, 1963) was an Austrian-born chemist and industrial executive in the American oil industry, and a controversial author. He may be best known for his provocative 1937 book on the assassination of Abraham Lincoln in which he proposed that a senior member of Lincoln's Cabinet orchestrated the plot to kill the president.

The South African Reserve Bank (SARB) is the central bank of South Africa. It was established in 1921 after Parliament passed an act, the "Currency and Bank Act of 10 August 1920", as a direct result of the abnormal monetary and financial conditions which World War I had brought. The SARB was only the fourth central bank established outside the United Kingdom and Europe, the others being the United States, Japan and Java. The earliest suggestions for the establishment of the Central Bank in South Africa date back to 1879. A select committee, of ten members of Parliament, was established on 31 March 1920 to examine the benefits to the national interest of the establishing of the central bank.

Following on the recommendations of the committee, the South African Reserve Bank opened for business on 30 June 1921, making it the oldest central bank in Africa. The first banknotes were issued to the public by the Bank on 19 April 1922. Set of ZAR notes 2012 to present R 104 000 000 000.00 and Set of ZAR notes 2018 to present 400 000 000 print.

Unlike the Bank of England, which provided the model for establishing the SARB, the SARB is privately owned.

Rugby League Week (frequently abbreviated to RLW) was the highest selling Australian rugby league magazine, ahead of major competitor Big League. It was published weekly (on Wednesdays) during the Australian rugby league season, which runs from March to late September (roughly corresponding to the southern hemisphere autumn and winter). The magazine was headquartered in Sydney.

The Anthony Massad Three-Decker is a historic triple-decker house in Worcester, Massachusetts. Built about 1912, it was cited as a good local example of Colonial Revival styling when it was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1990. Many details, including Tuscan columns on the porch and modillion blocks in the eaves, have been lost or obscured by subsequent exterior changes (see photo).

Boris Nikolaevich Chichkov (Борис Николаевич Чичков), born 1955 in Novokuznetsk is a German-Russian Physicist whose research focus is on the development of novel laser technologies and their applications in material processing, photonics, and biomedicine.
Flight Sergeant William Thomas Smith DCM, MM (21 October 1896 – October 1994) was a British World War I flying ace credited with five aerial victories.

"Take Me to the Pilot" is a song written by English musician Elton John and songwriter Bernie Taupin, and performed by John. It was originally released on John's eponymous second album in 1970.

It was recorded at Trident Studios in London in January 1970 and released in the United States in October 1970 as the A-side of a single, with "Your Song" as the B-side. Both songs received airplay, but "Your Song" was preferred by disc jockeys, becoming the singer's first hit (on both sides of the Atlantic) and rendering "Take Me To The Pilot" as the B-side.

The country duo Brothers Osborne recorded a remake of the song for the 2018 tribute album Restoration: Reimagining the Songs of Elton John and Bernie Taupin.

Yawalapiti (Jaulapiti) is an Arawakan language of Brazil. The Agavotaguerra (Agavotoqueng) reportedly spoke the same language.
